## 2. MLGen: Generative Design Framework Based on Machine Learning and Topology Optimization
Valid Text Names:
'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 1-2', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 2-4', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 4-6', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 6-8', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 8-9', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 9-15', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 15-16', 'kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign pages 16-17'

BibTex:
@article{kallioras2021mlgengenerativedesign,
    author = "Kallioras, Nikos Ath. and Lagaros, Nikos D.",
    title = "MLGen: Generative Design Framework Based on Machine Learning and Topology Optimization",
    year = "2021",
    journal = "Applied Sciences",
    volume = "11",
    pages = "12044",
    month = "Dec",
    doi = "10.3390/app112412044",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/app112412044",
    publisher = "MDPI AG",
    issue = "24",
    issn = "2076-3417"
}


Abstract:
Design and manufacturing processes are entering into a new era as novel methods and techniques are constantly introduced. Currently, 3D printing is already established in the production processes of several industries while more are continuously being added. At the same time, topology optimization has become part of the design procedure of various industries, such as automotive and aeronautical. Parametric design has been gaining ground in the architectural design literature in the past years. Generative design is introduced as the contemporary design process that relies on the utilization of algorithms for creating several forms that respect structural and architectural constraints imposed, among others, by the design codes and/or as defined by the designer. In this study, a novel generative design framework labeled as MLGen is presented. MLGen integrates machine learning into the generative design practice. MLGen is able to generate multiple optimized solutions which vary in shape but are equivalent in terms of performance criteria. The output of the proposed framework is exported in a format that can be handled by 3D printers. The ability of MLGen to efficiently handle different problems is validated via testing on several benchmark topology optimization problems frequently employed in the literature.

    publisher = "EWA Publishing",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "2755-2721"
}


Abstract:
Abstract. Additive manufacturing (AM) through generative design and topology optimisation creates complex, lightweight structures with exceptional material efficiency and structural integrity. When coupled with deep learning functionality, generative design and topology optimisation can explore broader design spaces and optimise more efficiently, creating novel AM structures that utilise material more efficiently and have better strength and performance than their counterparts created through conventional AM methods. The study tackles how deep learning models such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can be integrated into generative design and topology optimisation and how these integration help optimise material usage, production time and performance. Case studies from the aerospace, automotive, and healthcare industries exemplify how these synergies resulted in more resilient, cost-effective designs that would not have been possible through conventional AM approaches. The study focuses on material usage efficiency, reduction in production time and performance improvement to showcase how deep learning integrations enhance the process from design conceptualisation, through iterations, to final production.

## 4. A Machine Learning Approach for Mechanical Component Design Based on Topology Optimization Considering the Restrictions of Additive Manufacturing
Valid Text Names:
'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 1-2', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 2-3', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 3-5', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 5-7', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 7-9', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 9-12', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 12-15', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 15-18', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 18-20', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 20-22', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 22-24', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 24-25', 'ullah2024amachinelearning pages 25-26'

BibTex:
@article{ullah2024amachinelearning,
    author = "Ullah, Engr and Asami, Mohammad Karim and Holtz, Lukas and Röver, Tim and Azher, Kashif and Bartsch, Katharina and Emmelmann, Claus",
    title = "A Machine Learning Approach for Mechanical Component Design Based on Topology Optimization Considering the Restrictions of Additive Manufacturing",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "Journal of Manufacturing and Materials Processing",
    month = "Oct",
    doi = "10.3390/jmmp8050220",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.3390/jmmp8050220",
    publisher = "MDPI AG"
}


Abstract:
Additive manufacturing (AM) and topology optimization (TO) emerge as vital processes in modern industries, with broad adoption driven by reduced expenses and the desire for lightweight and complex designs. However, iterative topology optimization can be inefficient and time-consuming for individual products with a large set of parameters. To address this shortcoming, machine learning (ML), primarily neural networks, is considered a viable tool to enhance topology optimization and streamline AM processes. In this work, a machine learning (ML) model that generates a parameterized optimized topology is presented, capable of eliminating the conventional iterative steps of TO, which shortens the development cycle and decreases overall development costs. The ML algorithm used, a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) known as Pix2Pix-GAN, is adopted to train using a variety of training data pairs consisting of color-coded images and is applied to an example of cantilever optimization, significantly enhancing model accuracy and operational efficiency. The analysis of training data numbers in relation to the model’s accuracy shows that as data volume increases, the accuracy of the model improves. Various ML models are developed and validated in this study; however, some artefacts are still present in the generated designs. Structures that are free from these artefacts achieve 91% reliability successfully. On the other hand, the images generated with artefacts may still serve as suitable design templates with minimal adjustments. Furthermore, this research also assesses compliance with two manufacturing constraints: the limitations on build space and passive elements (voids). Incorporating manufacturing constraints into model design ensures that the generated designs are not only optimized for performance but also feasible for production. By adhering to these constraints, the models can deliver superior performance in future use while maintaining practicality in real-world applications.

## 7. Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology
Valid Text Names:
'watson2023generativedesignof pages 1-2', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 2-4', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 4-6', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 6-8', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 8-10', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 10-12', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 12-14', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 14-17', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 17-19', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 19-20', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 20-21', 'watson2023generativedesignof pages 21-21'

BibTex:
@article{watson2023generativedesignof,
    author = "Watson, Marcus and Leary, Martin and Downing, David and Brandt, Milan",
    title = "Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology",
    volume = "127",
    pages = "4619-4639",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1007/s00170-023-11691-9",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "9-10",
    issn = "0268-3768"
}


Abstract:
A generative design methodology is presented that solves for minimum volume and compliance space-frame systems, with consideration of stress and buckling constraints. The solution space is explored using formal topology optimisation routines. A parameterisation method converts voxelised topology optimisation solutions into skeletonised connectivity representations. An inequality constrained gradient descent optimisation method optimises and defines cross-sectional geometry. This enables fast and automatic solution generation, providing designers with sets of high-performing problem solutions. Skeleton representations provide an inexpensive modelling tool for parallel analysis of physical problems difficult to model using topology optimisation. Geometry is represented using traditional engineering cross-sections with well understood behaviour. This improves certainty in the performance of solutions, simplifying certification processes. The generative design of a structural aerospace bracket for additive manufacture has been used as a case study within this research. A design of experiments produced 360 topology optimisation results, altering input variables and discretisation resolution to identify their effect on solution outcomes and the performance of parameterisation. The proposed method was found to robustly generate high-performing solutions utilising low-resolution topology optimisation. Additionally, 6 high-performing topologies were identified, providing designers with a set of solutions to select from. Limitations on the parameterisation process were identified, with topology optimisation solutions with volume fractions above 0.2 not parameterising successfully, and simulations with a resolution of 5 mm element size and below generating low performing skeletonised topologies.

## 8. Performance-Driven Engineering Design Approaches Based on Generative Design and Topology Optimization Tools: A Comparative Study

}


Abstract:
The advent of Additive Manufacturing (AM) is uncovering the limits of the current CAD systems and, at the same time, is highlighting the potentials of the Topology Optimization (TO) and Generative Design (GD) tools that had not been fully exploited until now. Differently from the traditional design approach in which designers occupy a predominant role in each stage of the design process, the introduction of such tools in the product development process pushes toward simulation-driven design approaches which imply a significant change in the role of the designer. To this end, the paper presents a comparison of two different design methods for Additive Manufacturing based on the adoption of TO and GD tools. The comparison aims to offer a reflection on the evolution of the traditional approach when TO and GD tools are used, and to highlight the potential and limitations of these optimization tools when adopted in an integrated manner with the CAD systems. Furthermore, this comparative study can be a useful and practical source for designers to identify the most appropriate approach to adopt based on their needs and project resources. The comparative study is carried out through the design study of a prototype of a rocker arm and a brake pedal for the Formula Student race car. Their results, compared in terms of mechanical performances, show that both TO and especially GD tools can be efficiently adopted early in a design process oriented to AM to redesign components to make them lighter and stronger.

Abstract:
This review explores how generative design is combined with machine learning (ML) to achieve additive manufacturing (AM) and its societal transformative effect. Generative design uses complex algorithms to automate the process of designing best-fit designs, mass customization, and customization to suit specific customer requirements with high efficiency and quality. The scalability and predictability of artificial intelligence (AI) models make handling huge data easy and enable scale-up of production without compromising quality. This paper also focuses on how generative design can help accelerate innovation and product creation because it empowers designers to play in a wider space of design and provide solutions that cannot be reached with traditional techniques. AI integration with existing production processes is also vital to real-time manufacturing optimization—further increasing overall operational effectiveness. Additionally, the emergence of sophisticated predictive models like gradient boosting regression shows how ML can enable better accuracy and robustness of 3D printing operations to achieve quality standards of the outputs. This paper ends with what generative design and ML hold for the future of AM and how designing continues to be improved and modified to match changing industry requirements.

Abstract:
An analysis of topology optimization employing deep learning, namely Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), and topology optimization utilizing the Solid Isotropic Material with Penalization (SIMP) method is presented in this research. We describe the theoretical foundations of GANs and the SIMP technique. A cantilever beam with predetermined boundary conditions was the topic of a static study to show the practical efficacy of these methods. The structural performance parameters, such as maximal directional displacement, maximal Von Mises stress, and deformation energy. The findings show that deep learning-based topology optimization, as demonstrated by TopologyGAN, provides considerable benefits in terms of improved design correctness and computing performance.

function.arguments: {"question": "What are the key capabilities, methods, datasets, and limitations of generative CAD, text-to-CAD, and LLM-based CAD code generation systems? Include details about DeepCAD dataset, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, Text2CAD benchmark, ABC dataset, and evaluation metrics."}
function.name: gather_evidence

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 3-4'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The paper surveys generative CAD approaches, available datasets, and how large pretrained models enable text-to-CAD generation. Early CAD datasets were largely static B-rep models without modeling steps; more recent datasets include command sequences and paired natural-language annotations. Representative datasets and sizes include ABC (B-rep, 1,000,000+), SketchGraphs (command sequences, 15,000,000+), DeepCAD (command sequences, ~178k models), Fusion360 Gallery (command sequences, 8,625) and Text2CAD (extends DeepCAD with natural-language; resulting corpus ~170k models and >600k prompts). Methods differ by input modality (text, images, sketches, point clouds) and output representation (command sequences, B-rep, or script-based targets like CadQuery). Prior symbolic-code efforts include Query2CAD (57 samples, FreeCAD macros) and LLM4CAD (~5,000 CadQuery samples but limited to five part types). The authors emphasize using text-only inputs and leveraging foundation LLMs (GPT-4, Gemini) for spatial reasoning and code synthesis, generating accurate and executable CadQuery code. Evaluation focuses on executability and scaling behavior across model sizes; limitations include small or narrow datasets and early-stage, not-yet-specialized models, motivating larger, more diverse annotations and systematic evaluation.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 1-2'.

Jianxing Liao, Junyan Xu, Yatao Sun, Maowen Tang, Sicheng He, Jingxian Liao, Shui Yu, Yun Li, and Hongguan Xiao. Automated cad modeling sequence generation from text descriptions via transformer-based large language models. ArXiv, May 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.19490. This article has 8 citations.

The excerpt describes methods, datasets, and limitations relevant to generative CAD and text-to-CAD. DeepCAD uses a Transformer to generate CAD operation sequences, decomposing modeling into sketches and extrusion steps but producing disconnected sequences and supporting only basic operations. Text2CAD generates CAD sequences from text and visual features. Dataset-wise, ModelNet provides large 3D mesh models but lacks process data; the Fusion 360 Gallery adds modeling history and assembly data; the Mechanical Components Benchmark supplies complete modeling histories for mechanical parts but lacks natural language descriptions. The authors propose a CAutoD framework using LLMs and VLLMs to produce parameters and appearance descriptions, a Transformer-based CAD generator (TCADGen) with dual-channel feature aggregation, and CADLLM to refine sequences using TCADGen confidence scores. Key limitations noted: LLM-synthesized design data often lacks sufficient accuracy, LLMs are computationally expensive and inefficient, and it is difficult to guide LLMs with simple language or visuals to produce precise, editable CAD models. The excerpt does not provide details on SketchGraphs, ABC dataset specifics, the Text2CAD benchmark design, or concrete evaluation metrics.


---

## pqac-00000002

From text 'kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29'.

R Kilpeläinen. Utilizing generative ai in 3d modeling for robotics. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes sequence-based CAD generation (DeepCAD) and Transformer/LLM-driven approaches. DeepCAD’s dataset contains over 170,000 CAD models with construction sequences, enabling fully parametric, editable models with valid CAD features; however, it excludes some features (e.g., fillets) and cannot guarantee topological validity, especially for long/complex sequences. Text2CAD extends DeepCAD by annotating models with multiple natural-language prompts (via an LLM+VLM pipeline) and trains a Transformer to map text to construction sequences; its performance is limited by dataset biases toward simple shapes and missing feature types. LLM-based code generation writes scripts for systems like OpenSCAD and CadQuery; models (e.g., GPT-4) show surprising zero-shot competence for simple objects but struggle with complex geometry (examples: hex nut -> circular profile, missing flange boreholes, incorrect spring helix) due to lack of geometric reasoning. Improvement strategies include visual-feedback/refinement loops such as CADCodeVerify (VLM renders model, iteratively questions and refines code) and hybrid workflows where GPT-4 produces design docs, structured JSON, and selects scripting functions, with final rendering in OpenSCAD.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2'.

S Hou, L Chen, R Johnson, RS Makhija, and Y Ye. Obj2cad: object-oriented text-to-cad generation with large language models. Unknown journal, Unknown year.

The excerpt frames text-to-CAD as generating CAD models from natural-language descriptions and critiques the dominant procedural paradigm (e.g., DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery) which records long operation sequences from CAD logs. Procedural methods are brittle: they are strictly order-dependent, prone to cumulative errors and cascading dependencies, and often include complex shapes that are hard to describe, reducing interpretability and evaluability. Procedural datasets also encode many low-level geometric constraints (parallelism, concentricity, symmetry) that are useful in interactive tools but largely redundant for code generation; instead, high-level semantic constraints (e.g., “two holes must be concentric”) better capture design logic. The paper proposes OBJ2CAD and a curated 1,000-example dataset emphasizing object-oriented, hierarchical representations that de-emphasize low-level constraints and retain semantic relations. Methodologically, it uses an LLM-driven pipeline combining top-down planning (task decomposition) with bottom-up generation (object instantiation), adds geometric assembly reasoning by casting assembly as explicit geometric-mathematical constraints, and supports an interactive iterative mechanism for user feedback and object-graph expansion. It also notes that LLMs exhibit object-centric reasoning, aligning with the proposed paradigm.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocada pages 1-2'.

S Hou, L Chen, R Johnson, RS Makhija, and Y Ye. Obj2cad: object-oriented text-to-cad generation with large language models. Unknown journal, Unknown year.

The excerpt contrasts prior procedural text-to-CAD approaches with a proposed object-oriented alternative. Prior systems commonly use datasets derived from logged modeling sessions (cited examples: DeepCAD and Fusion 360 Gallery) and represent models as long ordered operation sequences. This procedural paradigm is described as brittle: strictly order-dependent, prone to cumulative errors and cascading dependencies, cluttered with redundant low-level geometric constraints (e.g., parallelism, concentricity, symmetry), and containing many shapes that are hard to describe in natural language. The authors argue that high-level semantic constraints (e.g., “two holes must be concentric”) are more valuable than low-level constraints for text-driven generation. To address these limitations, they introduce OBJ2CAD, including a curated object-oriented dataset of 1,000 examples, an LLM-driven framework combining top-down planning with bottom-up generation, explicit geometric assembly reasoning (formulating assembly as geometric-mathematical constraints), and an interactive iterative mechanism for user feedback and object-graph expansion. The excerpt does not cover SketchGraphs, Text2CAD benchmark, ABC dataset, or specific evaluation metrics.


---

## pqac-00000005

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

This excerpt describes Text-to-CadQuery, a paradigm that generates executable CadQuery code directly from natural language. The authors release a large dataset of 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and show that using CadQuery as the output simplifies the pipeline and enables fine-tuning pretrained language models rather than training task-specific models. They evaluate models (ranging from ~124M up to 7B and including a fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini) using traditional core metrics plus two supplementary geometric metrics: a point-based F1 (10,000 sampled surface points, threshold τ=0.02) and volumetric IoU (voxelized in unit cube at resolution 0.02). Findings include strong scaling from small to 3B models (Qwen2.5-3B attains top F1/IoU medians), but the 7B Mistral underperforms in accuracy—likely due to insufficient data—while achieving the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32). A fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini produced 0% Invalid Rate, showing larger models can produce fully executable code but still need adequate task data for precise 3D generation. Limitations noted: data scale/annotation effort, underfitting for larger models, and the need to test other input modalities (images, sketches, point clouds) with CadQuery outputs.


---

## pqac-00000006

From text 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3'.

Haoyang Xie and Feng Ju. Text-to-cadquery: a new paradigm for cad generation with scalable large model capabilities. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507. This article has 29 citations.

The excerpt proposes a Text-to-CadQuery paradigm: generate executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language rather than intermediate, task-specific command sequences. CadQuery is advantageous because it is a high-level, pure-Python CAD API executable without external software, making outputs both runnable and interpretable. The authors augment the DeepCAD/Text2CAD data by annotating samples with CadQuery, producing ~170,000 text–CadQuery pairs to fine-tune pretrained LLMs. They fine-tune six models (124M–7B parameters) and observe a clear scaling trend: larger models yield better, executable CAD code. Reported improvements include a top-1 exact match increase (best model 69.3% vs 58.8%) and a 48.6% reduction in Chamfer Distance. The paper contrasts this code-first approach with prior methods that output command sequences or B-rep representations, which require task-specific decoders and added pipeline complexity. It also highlights that foundation models’ strengths in Python generation and spatial reasoning make this direct-code approach effective. The excerpt notes limitations are discussed later but does not provide details here. The excerpt does not include specific information about Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, ABC dataset, or a comprehensive list of evaluation metrics beyond exact match and Chamfer Distance.


---

## pqac-00000007

From text 'fan2025caddesignerconceptualdesign pages 8-9'.

Fengxiao Fan, Jingzhe Ni, Xiaolong Yin, Sirui Wang, Xingyu Lu, Qiang Zou, Ruofeng Tong, Min Tang, and Peng Du. Caddesigner: conceptual design of cad models based on general-purpose agent. ArXiv, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2508.01031, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2508.01031. This article has 3 citations.

The excerpt compares several generative CAD and text-to-CAD approaches and highlights key methods, capabilities, evaluation setup, and limitations. Methods: Text2CAD is an end-to-end transformer-based auto-regressive model for parametric CAD from text; cadrille uses a two-stage pipeline (supervised fine-tuning on procedurally generated data + RL fine-tuning with online feedback); CADCodeVerify employs an agent using validation questions and visual feedback. Capabilities: CADDesigner generates structurally accurate models that better match image inputs by using expressive, high-level CAD operations (e.g., revolve, arrays/patterns) versus low-level extrusion-only operations. Evaluation: experiments used 1k samples from a filtered test set with abstract text prompts and image-based comparisons; metrics cited include SUC score and prompt-result alignment (CADDesigner outperforms others). Limitations: current systems struggle with precise global geometric constraints, spatial/topological reasoning, and complex mathematical geometry (e.g., involute gears); LLM/VLM and code-generation models have limited ability to produce correct spatial calculations and globally consistent designs. The excerpt does not provide details about DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, the ABC dataset, or a full Text2CAD benchmark description.

What are their sizes, representations, and how are they used as benchmarks?", "text_name_focus_list": ["wu2021deepcadadeep pages 1-2", "willis2021fusion360gallery pages 1-2", "heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 1-2", "lin2025asurveyon pages 1-3", "para2021sketchgengeneratingconstrained pages 1-3"]}

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000008

From text 'lin2025asurveyon pages 11-13'.

Ruiquan Lin, Yunwei Ji, Wanting Ding, Tianxiang Wu, Yaosheng Zhu, and Mengxi Jiang. A survey on deep learning in 3d cad reconstruction. Applied Sciences, 15:6681, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/app15126681, doi:10.3390/app15126681. This article has 9 citations.

From the excerpt: ABC (2019) is a very large CAD benchmark ("more than 1 million individual and high-quality CAD models") with parametric descriptions of surfaces and curves that "can generate data in different formats and resolutions so that various geometric learning algorithms can be compared fairly" (download: https://deep-geometry.github.io/abc-dataset). DeepCAD (2021) contains 178,238 items and is annotated with "sequence annotation" for procedural/operation-sequence tasks (download: https://github.com/ChrisWu1997/DeepCAD). The Fusion 360 Gallery set is provided in two related releases: a Reconstruction split (2021) with 8,625 items and "Build history" annotations, and an Assembly split (2022) listing "8251 Assemblies /154,468 Parts" with "Assembly information"; both are available at the Autodesk AILab GitHub (https://github.com/AutodeskAILab/Fusion360GalleryDataset). SketchGraphs (2020) supplies sketches and "diagram annotation" and is indicated as having sequence/sketch annotations (download: https://github.com/PrincetonLIPS/SketchGraphs). The excerpt presents these datasets as sources for CAD-focused learning tasks and benchmarks—ABC explicitly for fair comparisons across geometric learning methods, Fusion360 for reconstruction/assembly and build-history tasks, DeepCAD for sequence-based annotations, and SketchGraphs for sketch/diagram-based learning.


---

## pqac-00000009

From text 'lin2025asurveyon pages 13-14'.

Ruiquan Lin, Yunwei Ji, Wanting Ding, Tianxiang Wu, Yaosheng Zhu, and Mengxi Jiang. A survey on deep learning in 3d cad reconstruction. Applied Sciences, 15:6681, Jun 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.3390/app15126681, doi:10.3390/app15126681. This article has 9 citations.

From the excerpt: DeepCAD is a large CAD command-sequence dataset (178,238 CAD models) where each model is represented as a CAD command sequence; it was created to train an autoencoder and its scale and command-sequence representation make it suitable as a benchmark for learning generative or reconstruction models that require operation histories. The Fusion 360 Gallery reconstruction dataset is an artificial-design dataset (8625 design sequences/CAD programs) using B-Rep and recording clear construction history (sketch+extrude workflows), intended for ML on parametric CAD reconstruction. The Fusion 360 Gallery assembly dataset provides assembly-level data (8251 assemblies, 154,468 parts) plus joint data (32,148 joints between 23,029 parts) following the Fusion 360 API; its joint/constraint annotations make it a benchmark for assembly and connection-understanding tasks. SketchGraphs contains 15 million parametric CAD sketches extracted from Onshape, each with a geometric constraint graph; it is aimed at sketch-level, parametric tasks (e.g., patch segmentation, normal estimation). By contrast, the ABC dataset provides B‑Rep geometry but lacks operation/command sequences, limiting its use for benchmarks that require construction histories.


---

## pqac-00000010

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 5-6'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes three major CAD datasets used in geometric deep learning. ABC: a large-scale, real-world B-Rep-style dataset with over 1 million high-quality 3D CAD models. Models are represented as precisely parameterized curves and surfaces (suitable for sharp feature representation, patch segmentation, analytic differential measurements, and shape reconstruction). ABC models were collected via an openly accessible Onshape interface and include a geometry-processing pipeline for ML use, but construction history is only accessible via Onshape’s proprietary API (making reconstruction tasks harder). SketchGraphs: a collection of 15 million human-designed 2D sketches from Onshape, where each sketch is represented as a geometric constraint graph (nodes = 2D primitives, edges = constraints like coincidence/tangency). It is targeted for tasks such as sketch auto-completion, autoconstraint prediction, CAD inference from images, and learning semantic sketch representations; an open-source Python data-processing pipeline is provided. Fusion 360 Gallery: introduced by Autodesk as the first human-designed 3D CAD dataset that includes 3D operation sequences for programmatic CAD construction; it ships with an open-source Python environment (Fusion360 Gym) for processing CAD operation sequences. The excerpt does not mention DeepCAD. Note: many large-scale CAD databases (e.g., ABC) are predominantly unlabeled, which limits some supervised benchmark uses.


---

## pqac-00000011

From text 'willis2021fusion360gallery pages 1-2'.

Karl D. D. Willis, Yewen Pu, Jieliang Luo, Hang Chu, Tao Du, Joseph G. Lambourne, Armando Solar-Lezama, and Wojciech Matusik. Fusion 360 gallery. ACM Transactions on Graphics, 40:1-24, Jul 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3450626.3459818, doi:10.1145/3450626.3459818. This article has 340 citations and is from a highest quality peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt documents the Fusion 360 Gallery reconstruction dataset and its associated environment. The dataset contains 8,625 human-designed CAD programs encoded as construction sequences in a compact domain-specific language centered on sketch and extrude operations. The authors provide the Fusion 360 Gym, an execution environment that runs this sketch+extrude language and exposes the sequential construction as a Markov decision process, returning a geometric data structure as feedback after each operation. The work frames a standardized CAD reconstruction benchmark: recovering a CAD program from target geometry, and reports applying neurally-guided search (a learning-based program synthesis approach) to produce results on real-world data. The excerpt does not include details about other CAD datasets (DeepCAD, SketchGraphs, ABC) such as their sizes, representations, or benchmark usage, so those items are not covered here.


---

## pqac-00000012

From text 'heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 6-7'.

Negar Heidari and Alexandros Iosifidis. Geometric deep learning for computer-aided design: a survey. IEEE Access, 13:119305-119334, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2402.17695. This article has 47 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes the Fusion 360 Gallery as a user-contributed CAD repository introduced by Autodesk, presenting it as the first human-designed 3D CAD dataset with 3D operation sequences and an associated open-source Python environment called Fusion 360 Gym for preparing CAD operations for ML. It states the dataset contains both 2D and 3D geometric CAD data collected from Autodesk Online Gallery and notes datasets derived from it (for reconstruction, segmentation, assembly) are based on roughly 20,000 real-world designs. The table entries show specific derived Fusion 360 benchmark datasets: Fusion 360 Segmentation (35,680 models, B-Rep and mesh available, used for segmentation across 8 categories) and Fusion 360 Assembly (154,468 models, B-Rep and mesh available, used for joint prediction). The excerpt also remarks that public CAD databases like ABC are predominantly unlabeled. The excerpt does not provide details about DeepCAD or SketchGraphs (sizes, representations, or benchmark usage) beyond the brief mention of ABC being unlabeled.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000013

From text 'kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29'.

R Kilpeläinen. Utilizing generative ai in 3d modeling for robotics. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes capabilities and limits of LLM-driven CAD generation. Capabilities: generating CAD construction sequences yields fully parametric models whose geometries can be "clean, precise and have valid CAD features" and thus are editable in CAD tools; LLMs can produce syntactically correct scripting code for systems like OpenSCAD/CadQuery and show "surprising competence, even in zero-shot settings" for simple objects; LLMs can be embedded in workflows (e.g., GPT-4 performing "parametric computation, instruction sequence construction, and coding model scripting") and select library functions to produce renderable scripts. Limitations: datasets (DeepCAD) omit some features and are biased toward simple shapes, leading to missing features ("excludes certain CAD features") and manufacturability omissions; LLMs hallucinate or simplify geometry (e.g., "hexagonal nut... may incorrectly generate a circular profile"), lack geometric reasoning, and can produce invalid or topologically incorrect models ("Not every sequence of CAD operations results in a valid 3D model" and "the likelihood of generating invalid models increase with longer and more complex command sequences"). Agentic design loops with visual feedback (e.g., CADCodeVerify) allow VLM-based inspection and iterative correction ("visually inspect the generated model and provide corrective feedback"), but remain constrained by language variability and iteration limits.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'fan2025caddesignerconceptualdesign pages 8-9'.

Fengxiao Fan, Jingzhe Ni, Xiaolong Yin, Sirui Wang, Xingyu Lu, Qiang Zou, Ruofeng Tong, Min Tang, and Peng Du. Caddesigner: conceptual design of cad models based on general-purpose agent. ArXiv, Aug 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2508.01031, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2508.01031. This article has 3 citations.

The excerpt describes current capabilities and clear limits of LLM/VLM-based CAD generation. Capabilities: models can produce parametric CAD from text (Text2CAD), and pipelines combine supervised pretraining with reinforcement learning or online feedback (cadrille) and agentic verification with visual/QA loops (CADCodeVerify). Supporting expressive, high-level CAD operations (revolve, arrays, patterned constructs) and semantically aligned APIs yields structurally accurate, compact, and editable parametric models and better prompt-result alignment (as in CADDesigner). Limitations: LLMs/VLMs have limited spatial and topological reasoning, causing hallucinated or globally inconsistent features (e.g., a handle inside a cup) even when local dimensions are correct. They often produce syntactically valid but semantically irrelevant geometries when generalization fails. Generating models that require complex mathematical computations, meshing, or strength/manufacturability validation (e.g., involute gears) remains poor because code-generation cannot reliably compute required geometric parameters. Agentic design loops with visual feedback and verification help improve fidelity but do not fully solve abstraction and engineering-level accuracy issues. Overall, expressive CAD primitives and verification loops improve results, but manufacturability, global consistency, and complex engineering computations remain open challenges.


---

## pqac-00000015

From text 'kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 21-25'.

R Kilpeläinen. Utilizing generative ai in 3d modeling for robotics. Unknown journal, 2025.

The excerpt describes current AI capabilities and limits for CAD generation relevant to LLM-based approaches. Major vendors are embedding AI to automate tasks (e.g., AutoConstraint) and research projects (Autodesk’s Project Bernini) can generate 3D shapes from minimal inputs (images, text, voxels, point clouds). Research directions include LLMs and diffusion-based B-rep generative methods. A sequence-of-operations approach (DeepCAD) trains Transformers on construction sequences (trained on over 170,000 CAD models) and outputs parametric scripts that yield clean, precise geometries with valid CAD features; such outputs can be opened and modified in CAD software, supporting parametric editability. However, industry adoption today focuses more on augmenting traditional workflows than fully autonomous modeling. Accuracy and manufacturability concerns are highlighted by the mismatch between virtual validation and real-world performance—virtually approved grasps have failed physically, implying risks of hallucinated or non-manufacturable features and the need for physical validation. Overall, the text indicates promising parametric generative capabilities but also accuracy, validation, and practical deployment limitations.

The paper describes capabilities and partial solutions for LLM-based CAD generation alongside clear limitations. Capabilities: combining LLMs with vision-language models (VLLMs) enables generation of parameters and appearance descriptions via a semi-automated annotation pipeline; transformer-based generators (TCADGen) plus a refining CADLLM that uses confidence scores aim to produce and improve modeling sequences. Small, task-tuned generative modules can assist large models to improve detailed reasoning and reduce uncertainties. Limitations: outputs from LLMs frequently lack sufficient accuracy, making them prone to erroneous or unsupported design details (hallucination-like errors). LLMs are "computationally expensive and inefficient" and are hard to steer with simple language or visual prompts, which limits practical control. Converting synthesized design data into "high-quality, editable CAD models is difficult," a critical issue for manufacturing where "precision is criti- cal." Sequence-level problems (e.g., generating sketches before extrusion parameters, yielding disconnected sequences) and the current focus on data synthesis rather than parameter review or manufacturability validation remain open gaps. The paper suggests confidence-based refinement and VLLM integration as steps toward agentic, feedback-enabled workflows but does not claim full agentic visual-feedback loops or guaranteed manufacturability/parametric editability.

Generative models can produce CAD programs/command sequences and be enhanced by techniques like guided diffusion denoising in latent space plus regression-based self-repair to improve feasibility. A reported baseline failure mode is that roughly 10% of generated B-reps are infeasible; the self-repair pipeline converted two-thirds of those infeasible designs to feasible ones while "maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models." Key limitations stem from the nature of CAD: B-reps encode parametric surfaces, edges, and vertices, requiring editable and manufacturable representations and precise topological/parametric relationships, which are "challenging to process directly with standard neural network architectures." The domain also suffers from limited large-scale, structured datasets (though ABC contains ~1M B-reps), and the integration of generative AI with CAD modeling remains limited. The excerpt describes CADFusion, an LLM-based Text-to-CAD approach that combines sequential and visual signals. Capabilities: it uses pre-trained LLMs as a backbone, fine-tunes them on ground-truth parametric sequences, and incorporates visual feedback by scoring rendered outputs with an automated LVM-based pipeline. To handle the non-differentiable rendering step, the method frames learning as a preference task and adopts direct preference optimization (DPO), alternating between sequential learning and visual feedback stages to balance token-level sequence learning and global visual alignment. Limitations and failure modes noted in the text: the rendering pathway is non-differentiable, preventing direct backpropagation; auto-regressive sequential learning "emphasizes the local continuity between tokens but may not fully capture the global appearance of the CAD model"; the "many-to-one rendering characteristic" means "multiple parametric sequences can produce the same visual object," creating ambiguity. Consequently, "training solely on parametric sequences may cause the model to give more emphasis to those present in the training set, overlooking other valid ones," which can hurt parametric diversity, editability, and visual/semantic accuracy. The paper proposes visual-feedback loops to mitigate—but not fully eliminate—these limitations. ## pqac-00000021

From text 'wu2021deepcadadeep pages 1-2'.

Rundi Wu, Chang Xiao, and Changxi Zheng. Deepcad: a deep generative network for computer-aided design models. 2021 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), pages 6752-6762, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492. This article has 419 citations.

DeepCAD introduces the first deep generative model that explicitly outputs CAD construction sequences (commands plus parameters) rather than discrete shape representations. The authors unify commonly used CAD operations into a common structured representation that encodes command types, parameters (both discrete and continuous), and sequential order to handle CADs’ irregular, parametric, and order-dependent nature. Architecturally, they build a Transformer-based autoencoder that embeds CAD command sequences into a latent space and decodes latent vectors back into CAD command sequences, drawing an analogy between CAD sequences and natural language. To support training, they assembled and publicly released a large dataset of 178,238 CAD models paired with their construction sequences. The model supports both autoencoding and random generation of diverse, editable CAD designs that can be imported into CAD tools or converted to meshes/point clouds. Evaluation emphasizes generation quality and robustness via ablation studies; the paper highlights challenges from sequential/parametric aspects and situates the work relative to Transformer usage in related sequence-generation problems like DeepSVG.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'wu2021deepcadadeep pages 2-3'.

Rundi Wu, Chang Xiao, and Changxi Zheng. Deepcad: a deep generative network for computer-aided design models. 2021 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), pages 6752-6762, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492. This article has 419 citations.

DeepCAD (Wu et al., 2021) introduces a generative model for CAD construction sequences rather than B-rep geometry. The paper proposes a CAD sequence representation tailored for neural networks: models are expressed as ordered, parameterized commands (e.g., curve commands, extrudes, booleans) with loop markers (⟨SOL⟩) and sequence terminators (⟨EOS⟩). Curve commands focus on line/arc/circle (the three most common types, covering 92% of cases) and include normalized geometric parameters; a command sequence can be converted into a B-rep but not vice versa. DeepCAD uses a Transformer-based autoencoder to encode both command tokens and their sequential order (Sec. 3.2). The authors built a new, significantly larger CAD command dataset for training (Sec. 3.3) and define a training objective derived from the representation (Sec. 3.4). The excerpt documents representation design, dataset scale, and the Transformer autoencoder approach, but does not include implementation details (layer counts, latent sizes), exact loss functions, or the evaluation metrics used; those details are in other sections of the paper.

From the excerpt, DeepCAD (Wu et al., ICCV 2021) is presented as the first method to treat 3D shape generation as a sequence of CAD operations. The paper frames CAD modeling as sequences of commands and evaluates both command-level correctness and geometric fidelity. Key evaluation metrics reported include Chamfer Distance (CD) for geometric similarity, Command Accuracy (ACCcmd) and Parameter Accuracy (ACCparam) for operation and parameter correctness, and Invalid Ratio for proportion of generated models that fail to convert to valid point clouds. The authors introduced several representation and training variants: Alt-Rel (relative curve positioning), Alt-Trans (adds loop initial position and sketch plane origin), Alt-ArcMid (arc defined by start/end/mid points), Alt-Regr (direct regression of CAD parameters), and DeepCAD + Aug (data augmentation by randomly combining CAD command sequences). Quantitative results in the tables show high ACCcmd/ACCparam (≈99.3–99.5% and ≈97.3–97.98%), median CD values ~0.75–0.79, and low Invalid Ratios (2.72–3.51%). A separate comparison for unconditional generation reports coverage, MMD, JSD, Novel/Unique/Valid percentages versus BrepGen. The excerpt does not specify the dataset used nor details of a transformer architecture, so those elements are not available here.

## pqac-00000024

From text 'wu2021deepcadadeep pages 3-4'.

Rundi Wu, Chang Xiao, and Changxi Zheng. Deepcad: a deep generative network for computer-aided design models. 2021 IEEE/CVF International Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV), pages 6752-6762, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2105.09492. This article has 419 citations.

The excerpt describes DeepCAD’s CAD sequence representation and Transformer-based autoencoder. The paper models CAD designs as sequences of frequently-used commands (a subset listed in Table 1) comprising sketch and extrusion commands: sketches are closed 2D loops in 3D space and extrusion both lifts profiles into 3D and specifies boolean merge operations. A CAD model M is a command sequence M = [C1,...,CNc] with each Ci = (ti, pi). Command parameters are stacked into a 16×1 vector pi = [x, y, α, f, r, θ, φ, γ, px, py, pz, s, e1, e2, b, u]. Extrusion defines the sketch plane orientation via rotation parameters (θ, γ, φ) and includes a scale factor s. Models are normalized into a 2×2×2 cube and sketch profiles to their bounding box; this enables quantizing continuous parameters into 256 levels (8-bit) to turn regression into classification, which helps preserve geometric relations. The representation is treated like language: the vocabulary is CAD commands in sequence. Architecturally, an encoder projects embedded command sequences to a latent z, and a decoder uses learned constant embeddings and attention to z to output the predicted command sequence.

The excerpt describes SketchGen as decomposing sketch generation into primitives and constraints via p(S)=p(C|P)p(P), implemented with two autoregressive Transformer networks: one for primitive generation and one for conditional constraint generation. Inputs are token sequences where primitives and constraints are concatenated and separated by special tokens Λ and Ω; parameters are quantized, embedded, and augmented with positional encodings. An interpreter converts Transformer raw outputs into Protocol Buffer (PB) triplets (di, ci, fi) and feeds interpretations back to guide subsequent generation. CAD as Language is noted to describe sketches as PB messages (triplets) and evaluated on a large, de-duplicated OnShape collection of over 4.7 million sketches. VITRUVION is presented as a recent autoregressive sketch generator that, like SketchGen, trains separate Transformer networks for primitives and constraints, but its key contribution is conditioning generation on additional contexts (e.g., hand-drawn sketches) to support reverse-engineering from noisy inputs. The excerpt notes that SketchGraphs provides rich 2D relational geometry but does not supply executable 3D feature sequences, so it cannot represent editable construction histories. To fill that gap the authors introduce HistCAD, a representation standard and dataset. HistCAD includes an executable intermediate language for parametric CAD that is CAD-backend independent and explicitly encodes sketch primitives, sketch constraints, feature operations, and boundary references. The representation flattens sketches and records explicit geometric and dimensional constraint graphs covering 19 constraint types, with a flattening process that removes redundant boundary serialization while preserving exact profile equivalence. The HistCAD dataset contains 170,236 executable sequences drawn from academic and industrial sources, aiming to unify constraint richness and industrial complexity. The paper also defines a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark with three metrics—Edit Reachability (ER), Conditional Preserved Constraint Satisfaction Rate (cPCSR), and Overall Editable Success (ER × cPCSR)—and provides experimental evidence that explicit constraints are necessary for preserving design intent after parameter edits and that industrial data improves generalization. The excerpt describes SketchGen and its evaluation dataset but does not mention Vitruvion or HistCAD. SketchGen is presented as a generative framework for constrained sketches and is evaluated on the Sketch-Graph dataset, described here as one of the largest publicly-available constrained sketch datasets that includes constraint information and a generative modeling framework. SketchGen addresses the heterogeneity of CAD representations by designing a well-structured sequential language/syntax for sketches so each sketch is a sequence of tokens; the syntax tree is used as guidance. The model uses a transformer architecture to capture long-range dependencies and jointly outputs primitives and their constraints. It builds on ideas like pointer networks to refer to previously generated elements. Continuous primitive parameters can be aggressively quantized during generation; a sampled graph can then be converted to a physical sketch by solving a constrained optimization with traditional solvers to remove quantization errors. The excerpt also highlights why naive alternatives (rasterization or fixed padding) lose constraint information or are inefficient.

The excerpt describes parametric CAD sketches as collections of geometric primitives (lines, circles, etc.) plus explicit constraints (e.g., parallelism, tangency, coincidence, rotational symmetry) that encode design intent and enable coherent edit propagation. Vitruvion is a generative model trained on the SketchGraphs dataset: it autoregressively samples sequences of primitives (with initial coordinates) and then samples constraints that reference those primitives. The model uses self-attention to propagate sketch state to a next-step prediction module; sampling iterates until a stop token is produced. Outputs are constraint-graph representations compatible with standard CAD software: the generated constraint graph is handed to a solver to identify the final geometric configuration, enabling import, solve, and subsequent editable modifications with propagated edits. SketchGraphs provides constraint supervision and consists of millions of real-world sketches paired with ground-truth geometric constraint graphs. The paper evaluates the model on tasks including autocomplete (completion from partial sketches), autoconstrain (inferring constraints for primitives), and image-conditional synthesis (inferring primitives and constraints from hand-drawn raster images).

The excerpt describes SketchGraphs as a large-scale dataset of 15 million real-world 2D CAD sketches from the Onshape platform, each stored as a geometric constraint graph (nodes = primitives, edges = designer-imposed relationships). An open-source processing pipeline is provided; the dataset includes underlying parametric geometry and ground-truth construction operations for both primitives and constraints, and the pipeline can produce noisy renderings for paired-data use cases. For generative, constraint-aware sketch modeling, CurveGen generates a sketch hypergraph by first sampling vertices V then hyperedges E conditioned on V (p(G)=p(E|V)p(V)), predicting precise primitive coordinates while implicitly encoding primitive types in hyperedges. Because CurveGen predicts coordinates directly and is independent of a constraint solver, constraints must be inferred as post-processing; this makes subsequent editing and propagation of constraint changes harder. CurveGen-TurtleGen addresses this by using a Turtle Graphics representation (pen-down/pen-draw/pen-up) and an autoregressive Transformer (TurtleGen) to generate drawing command sequences that form sketches. The excerpt describes HistCAD: a representation standard, dataset, and benchmark for executable parametric CAD with explicit constraints. HistCAD defines an intermediate, CAD-software-independent language that records sketch primitives, explicit geometric and dimensional constraints, feature operations, and 3D point boundary references (used to recover B-Rep entities at runtime). The dataset contains 170,236 executable sequences aligned with native CAD models, STEP exports, rendered views, and text annotations, combining academic-scale sources (DeepCAD), constraint-rich sketches (SketchGraphs), and professionally authored Fusion 360 programs to achieve industrial complexity. The dataset also supports supervised CAD generation from text and direct LLM workflows.

## pqac-00000032

From text 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 3-4'.

Ruiyu Wang, Yu Yuan, Shizhao Sun, and Jiang Bian. Text-to-cad generation through infusing visual feedback in large language models. ArXiv, Jan 2501. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2501.19054, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2501.19054. This article has 36 citations.

The excerpt describes CADFusion, a Text-to-CAD framework that infuses visual feedback into large language models. Key design choices: it uses Sketch-and-Extrude (SE) representations and models CAD parametric sequences as text tokens, and adopts LLMs as the backbone. CADFusion combines sequential and visual signals by alternating two training stages: a Sequential Learning (SL) stage that trains the model on ground-truth parametric sequences to learn sequence structure and parametric operations, and a Visual Feedback (VF) stage that trains the model to evaluate rendered outputs. In VF the system "rewards parametric sequences that render into visually preferred objects and penalizes those that do not," encouraging generations whose rendered object matches user intent. The authors implement an automatic scoring pipeline for CAD models using large vision models (LVMs) to produce preference data, and they use DPO/RLAIF-style RL on AI-generated feedback rather than costly human annotations. The alternating SL/VF procedure is intended to preserve contributions from both sequential and visual signals. The excerpt does not provide information about Query2CAD, CAD-Llama, or the Daareyni et al. 2025 paper.

The Daareyni et al. (2025) paper presents GAD, an open-source Python/Streamlit application that integrates large language models (via the OpenAI GPT API), OpenSCAD, and computer-vision techniques to generate editable CAD model scripts from multimodal inputs (voice, text, sketches/blueprints). Its stated objectives are translating multimodal inputs into error-free CAD, implementing a three-layer closed-loop feedback mechanism (syntax quality check, LLM self-evaluation/refine loop, and user feedback loop), and evaluating LLM-assisted CAD modeling (case study: gears and design-to-manufacturing). GAD’s architecture comprises a user-interface layer, a processing/LLM layer that constructs structured prompts and parses GPT responses, and a rendering/export layer supporting outputs like .stl and G-code and connections to 3D printers. The system emphasizes real-time script display for rapid iteration, educational use, and collaboration. The provided excerpt focuses on GAD and related prior work (e.g., ZOO text-to-CAD, Deng et al. using ChatGPT) but does not include details about CADFusion (Wang et al. 2025), Query2CAD, or CAD-Llama.

## pqac-00000034

From text 'li2505seekcadaselfrefined pages 1-2'.

Xueyang Li, Jiahao Li, Yu Song, Yunzhong Lou, and Xiangdong Zhou. Seek-cad: a self-refined generative modeling for 3d parametric cad using local inference via deepseek. ArXiv, May 2505. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.17702, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2505.17702. This article has 12 citations.

The excerpt describes Seek-CAD, a training-free generative CAD framework built on a locally deployed DeepSeek-R1-32B-Q4 model. Seek-CAD uses retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a local CAD code corpus to produce Python-like parametric CAD code. To improve alignment between generated code and target descriptions/geometric constraints, the method employs a step-wise visual feedback loop: it renders step-wise perspective images of the modeling process and uses a Vision-Language Model (Gemini-2.0) to evaluate those images against DeepSeek-R1 Chain-of-Thought (CoT) outputs. The visual + CoT feedback is incorporated iteratively to self-refine the CAD code and parameters. The paper also introduces the SSR (Sketch, Sketch-based feature, and Refinements) design paradigm for representing models as sequences of SSR triples, and a CapType reference mechanism to link sketch primitives to resulting topology. To support training-free generation and evaluation, the authors created a 40k-sample CAD dataset following the SSR paradigm, with textual descriptions generated by GPT-4o. Overall, Seek-CAD emphasizes locally runnable LLM inference, step-wise visual feedback for self-refinement, an SSR modeling representation, and a new dataset for parametric CAD generation.

      GenCAD: Image-Conditioned Computer-Aided Design Gen-
        eration with Transformer-Based Contrastive Representation
      and Diffusion Priors



       Md Ferdous Alam                                                                  mfalam@mit.edu
             Department of Mechanical Engineering
              Massachusetts Institute of Technology

           Faez Ahmed                                                                                 faez@mit.edu
             Department of Mechanical Engineering
              Massachusetts Institute of Technology2025


                                              AbstractApr
8
                 The creation of manufacturable and editable 3D shapes through Computer-Aided Design
                (CAD) remains a highly manual and time-consuming task, hampered by the complex topol-
                   ogy of boundary representations of 3D solids and unintuitive design tools.  While most
                  work in the 3D shape generation literature focuses on representations like meshes, voxels, or
                     point clouds, practical engineering applications demand the modifiability and manufactura-[cs.CV]                   bility of CAD models and the ability for multi-modal conditional CAD model generation.
                    This paper introduces GenCAD, a generative model that employs autoregressive transform-
                        ers with a contrastive learning framework and latent diffusion models to transform image
                     inputs into parametric CAD command sequences, resulting in editable 3D shape representa-
                        tions. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that GenCAD significantly outperforms existing
                        state-of-the-art methods in terms of the unconditional and conditional generations of CAD
                     models. Additionally, the contrastive learning framework of GenCAD facilitates the retrieval
                        of CAD models using image queries from large CAD databases, which is a critical challenge
                     within the CAD community. Our results provide a significant step forward in highlighting
                     the potential of generative models to expedite the entire design-to-production pipeline and
                      seamlessly integrate different design modalities.



         1  INTRODUCTIONarXiv:2409.16294v2





             Figure 1: GenCAD demonstrates generative CAD models conditioned on images or sketches, where the CAD
            model is generated sequentially using a language-like representation of CAD operations


                                                           1Generating 3D shapes is a fundamental part of the engineering design process that morphs the design from
creative intuition to digital 3D shapes. Professional engineers use modern Computer-Aided Design (CAD)
models to digitally represent 3D shapes for applications in automotive, aerospace, manufacturing, medical
devices, consumer products, and architectural designs. Engineering design of such 3D solid modeling is a
complex sequential process that requires human expertise and intuition. Since the invention of CAD, most
CAD modeling tasks have been manual and typically go through a long iterative process to finalize a design
with desired requirements.  Additionally, modern CAD software is highly non-intuitive for humans and
comes with a steep learning curve for most practitioners (Piegl, 2005). Based on the motivations of recent
literature, we argue that some portion of the 3D CAD modeling process can be automated using learning-
based approaches, which can offer tremendous acceleration in the design and manufacturing pipeline. An
important first step in this direction is to build generative models that can provide valid and real-world
3D CAD models based on user intuitions. As most of the generative model architectures are proposed for
image or text generation, a carefully designed framework is needed for 3D CAD modeling in engineering
computational design tasks.

Generative models, built upon large neural network architectures, have recently shown impressive perfor-
mance in image and text generation tasks (Ramesh et al., 2021; Razavi et al., 2019; Saharia et al., 2022;
Karras et al., 2019; Rombach et al., 2022). To align the outputs of these generative models with user in-
tentions, they are often conditioned on additional image or text input. Inspired by the success of text and
image modalities, many recent studies have focused on building generative models for 3D representations
such as meshes (Geuzaine & Remacle, 2009; Feng et al., 2019), voxels (Wang et al., 2017; Alam et al., 2025),
point clouds (Qi et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2019), and implicit representations (Jun & Nichol, 2023). These
applications do not include CAD modeling tasks that require editable and manufacturable representation of
3D solids. One of the fundamental challenges in building generative models for CAD is the underlying data
structure of the 3D solid model. Since the 1980s, the industry standard in Computer-Aided Design (CAD)
has been the use of boundary representations (B-rep) (Weiler, 1986) that encodes geometry information in
the parametric representation of surfaces, edges, and vertices. Although B-rep is more complex than other
types of 3D shape representations, it offers several benefits. For example, the boundary representation of the
3D solid model is not resolution-dependent and memory-intensive such as meshes, voxels, or point clouds
while also being easier to render, unlike their counterparts, such as implicit representations. Additionally,
the existing off-the-shelf geometry kernels can seamlessly convert a parametric CAD command sequence or
a CAD program1 into a B-rep, which makes this representation the de facto industry standard. However,
due to the complex topological relationship between geometric entities, B-rep data structure is not directly
suitable as input for neural network architectures, and an intermediate network-friendly representation must
be utilized. Few recent studies have developed CAD datasets that are neural network friendly, which makes
it feasible to build powerful generative models for directly generating B-reps or CAD models (Koch et al.,
2019; Wu et al., 2021; Willis et al., 2021; 2022). Most of these approaches utilize one of the following:
graph-based representation of 3D solid models (Jayaraman et al., 2021; Lambourne et al., 2021), a sequen-
tial representation of CAD modeling operations (Xu et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2021), or a specialized variant
of the B-rep such as indexed list data structure (Jayaraman et al., 2022).

Learning to generate the CAD modeling sequence can provide more usefulness than directly learning to
generate a B-rep. We argue that direct B-rep generation is less attractive as it does not encode the underlying
design history. The sequence of solid modeling operations, or CAD program, is critical to modern CAD
software and offers a more flexible and interpretable representation than the direct B-rep. More specifically,
a B-rep model can be thought of as a sequence of parametric CAD commands that can create a 3D solid
shape using an off-the-shelf geometry kernel. Although this representation of 3D CAD is straightforward and
scalable, most available literature focuses on the unconditional generation of CAD programs, which is not
aligned with any user input. We argue that these unconditional generative models lack the true potential
of generative CAD models for useful 3D solid modeling tasks. To align the generative CAD models with
user intention, we propose a new type of neural network-based architecture that is conditioned on CAD
images. The idea of image-to-CAD conversion or image-to-technical drawing conversion is of particular
importance to the design community (Dori & Tombre, 1995; Dori & Wenyin, 1999; Nagasamy & Langrana,


  1A sequence of parameterized CAD modeling operations is denoted as a CAD program


                                              21990; Gümeli et al., 2022; Majumdar & Seethalakshmy, 1997). Although several attempts have been made
in the past, most of the approaches use heuristics and are application-specific, which makes them difficult
to generalize. Our approach is largely motivated by the recent success of text-to-image and image-to-mesh
literature (Alliegro et al., 2023; Tyszkiewicz et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2024a).

Here we focus on the problem of learning to generate CAD programs in terms of parametric command
sequences based on CAD images. We propose a new generative model architecture, generative CAD (Gen-
CAD), that can sequentially generate an entire CAD program aligned with an input CAD-image as shown in
Figure 2. Our main idea is to learn the joint distribution of the latent representation of the CAD command
sequences and the CAD images or sketches using a contrastive learning-based approach. Next, we develop a

            Prashant Govindarajan*1,2,3, Davide Baldelli*1,2,3, Jay Pathak4, Quentin Fournier2, and Sarath

            Computer-aided design (CAD) is the digital construction of 2D and 3D objects, and is central to a wide range
              of engineering and manufacturing applications like automobile and aviation. Despite its importance, CAD
            modeling remains largely a time-intensive, manual task. Recent works have attempted to automate this process
             with small transformer-based models and handcrafted CAD sequence representations. However, there has been
                  little effort to leverage the potential of large language models (LLMs) for sequential CAD design. In this work,2025     we introduce a new large-scale dataset of more than 170k CAD models annotated with high-quality, human-
               like descriptions generated with our pipeline based on GPT-4.1. Using this dataset, we fine-tune powerful
            code-LLMs to generate CAD sequences represented in a JSON-based format from natural language descriptions,Sep
            demonstrating the viability and effectiveness of this approach for text-conditioned CAD generation. Because
            simple metrics often fail to reflect the quality of generated objects, we introduce geometric and topological30
             metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic to provide richer structural insights. Our
            experiments and ablation studies on both synthetic and human-annotated data demonstrate that CADmium
                 is able to automate CAD design, drastically speeding up the design of new objects. The dataset, code, and
              fine-tuned models are available online.[cs.GR]         Data and Model: huggingface.co/collections/chandar-lab/cadmium-6866b402be81f39321af98d4
            Code Repository: github.com/chandar-lab/cadmium


           1. Introduction

            Throughout history, humans have developed ever-more complex objects for survival, architectural, engineering,
           and artistic purposes. These range from early inventions such as hunting tools, wheels, and clocks, to modern
             systems such as space shuttles. With the emergence of computers, programs were written to process machining
              instructions, and soon after, to design 3D objects virtually. Computer-aided design (CAD) has since been
             widely adopted, significantly improving the speed and efficiency of the engineering and manufacturing process.
            However, the CAD pipeline still requires significant human effort and expertise.

            Recognizing that CAD is a sequential process involving 2D sketches, such as lines and circles, followed by 3D
             operations like extrusions, recent studies have leveraged transformer architectures and publicly available CADarXiv:2507.09792v2       datasets to automatically generate design steps in an autoregressive manner [Wu et al., 2021, Khan et al.,
             2024]. Recently, there has been a growing interest in generating 3D objects from natural language descriptions,
             as this approach would make CAD accessible to a broader, non-expert audience and speed up the creation
             process even further. Such an interest likely stems from the progress in large pre-trained foundation models
              that has resulted in tremendous reasoning and generative capabilities in natural language [Brown et al., 2020]
           and images [Esser et al., 2024]. However, unlike these modalities, text-conditioned CAD object generation has
             not seen the same success as the field still faces two key challenges: (1) the lack of high-quality, human-like
              textual annotations for CAD samples, and (2) the absence of suitable embeddings for CAD design histories that
                facilitate the effective use of pre-trained language models.

            Current approaches address the first challenge by automating text annotation using sophisticated state-of-
              the-art annotation pipelines with vision-language and mixture of experts models [Khan et al., 2024, Xu et al.,
           2024] and the second with vectorized representations of CAD sequences combined with separate embedding
