## 4. badagabettu2024query2cadgeneratingcad pages 1-3 (Context ID: pqac-00000038)

       QUERY2CAD: GENERATING CAD MODELS USING NATURAL
                      LANGUAGE QUERIES





                   Akshay Badagabettu             Sai Sravan Yarlagadda         Amir Barati Farimani
                     Mechanical Engineering            Mechanical Engineering           Mechanical Engineering
                    Carnegie Mellon University         Carnegie Mellon University        Carnegie Mellon University
                           Pittsburgh, PA 15213                 Pittsburgh, PA 15213                Pittsburgh, PA 15213
                 abadagab@andrew.cmu.edu      saisravy@andrew.cmu.edu          barati@cmu.edu2024



                                                           June 5, 2024May

                                     ABSTRACT31

                   Computer Aided Design (CAD) engineers typically do not achieve their best prototypes in a single
                        attempt. Instead, they iterate and refine their designs to achieve an optimal solution through multiple
                         revisions. This traditional approach, though effective, is time-consuming and relies heavily on the
                        expertise of skilled engineers. To address these challenges, we introduce Query2CAD, a novel
                    framework to generate CAD designs. The framework uses a large language model to generate[cs.LG]                executable CAD macros. Additionally, Query2CAD refines the generation of the CAD model with
                       the help of its self-refinement loops. Query2CAD operates without supervised data or additional
                           training, using the LLM as both a generator and a refiner. The refiner leverages feedback generated by
                        the BLIP2 model, and to address false negatives, we have incorporated human-in-the-loop feedback
                         into our system. Additionally, we have developed a dataset that encompasses most operations used
                         in CAD model designing and have evaluated our framework using this dataset. Our findings reveal
                          that when we used GPT-4 Turbo as our language model, the architecture achieved a success rate of
                  53.6% on the first attempt. With subsequent refinements, the success rate increased by 23.1%. In
                          particular, the most significant improvement in the success rate was observed with the first iteration
                       of the refinement. With subsequent refinements, the accuracy of the correct designs did not improve
                           significantly. We have open-sourced our data, model, and code.1


          1  Introduction

         CAD has revolutionized the way engineers, architects, and designers conceptualize and design prototypes and products.arXiv:2406.00144v1     CAD designing is a very important step to design prototypes and also it helps in understanding the potential failures that
             might occur during the manufacturing phase. Over the years, CAD softwares have evolved significantly and become
              very powerful. CAD designing was always done by a CAD engineer as it required a deep understanding of complex
               operations [1]. The reasoning capabilities of LLMs have simplified many complex problems [2]. However, using LLMs
                to aid in the generation of CAD models remains largely untapped. The integration of natural language processing into
         CAD can significantly improve the design time. It can also make complex CAD software more accessible to people
              with less experience in CAD designing.

            Over the past decade, deep learning methods had significant success in understanding data formats like mesh
                 [3], voxels [4], and point clouds.  With the advent of diffusion models, a significant shift occurred towards
              image-conditioned 3D generative modeling, particularly enhancing the capabilities in point clouds and neural fields
                 [5]. A critical limitation identified in these studies was the necessity for precise camera poses, which constrained the
                applicability and flexibility of the models. Recent innovations eliminated the need for camera poses [6], but the major
             drawback of such data format is the problems that it might pose during feature extraction. These extracted features are

                 1github.com/akshay140601/Query2CADFigure 1: Proposed architecture of Query2CAD. The user query is passed to an LLM that generates a Python macro to
generate the corresponding CAD model. The isometric view is captured, and refinement is performed if the VQA score
does not cross the threshold. The loop is run for a maximum of 3 times.


critical to the CAD model, and converting them to the right data format, such as triangular meshes, can be expensive
and can also lead to information loss [7].

In this work, we introduce Query2CAD, a novel architecture that returns a CAD model similar to the user’s
prompt. We plan to exploit the capabilities of language models to generate Python programs (Macro) that can be
executed in the FreeCAD software to obtain 3D CAD models. A designer refines his design multiple times based
on the feedback given to him until he designs a CAD model that closely aligns with what the user has asked for. To
mimic this behavior, our architecture has a refinement loop that refines its generation with respect to the user prompt.
We incorporated a caption-generating model to provide feedback on what was designed, and, to encounter the false
negatives of this model, we have also incorporated human-in-the-loop feedback. We have used Visual Question
Answering Score [8] to check if we have reached an optimal CAD design. At a high level, our architecture takes input
from the user (for instance, "Give me the CAD design of a water bottle") and passes it to a language model, which
generates a working CAD design. Throughout this process, users do not require any prior knowledge of CAD software
and can interact with the system using natural language. A brief overview of our architecture is shown in Figure 1.


2  Related works

Generating 3D models: Early explorations of generating 3D models used variational autoencoders [9] [10]. However,
this just worked for simple models, and the 3D models generated were very similar to that of the training corpus and
failed at generating models outside of the training corpus [11]. Later researchers have investigated 3D GANs [12] [13],
but the main drawback of generating 3D models using GAN is its instability during training [14]. However, recently
image conditioned 3D modeling has shown good results in generating 3D models[15] but the precision is not very good
when compared to that of a CAD model. In our research, we aim to advance beyond point cloud generation to focus on
3D CAD model creation. CAD models are pivotal to the manufacturing process and facilitate the direct transfer of
data to production machinery. While 3D models generated by various image-to-3D diffusion models have accurately
captured the geometry of points in the 3D CAD models [16], CAD models provide more profound utility by enabling
an advanced level of design, analysis, and manufacturability.

Innovative approaches taken by people that can assist in 3D CAD model generation:   Recent studies,
such as those by [17], have highlighted the capability of language models in generating conceptual designs. These
studies demonstrate that language models can effectively reason and produce detailed concept designs that can
significantly assist design engineers in their work. Some works [18] integrated imagery of the model into their 3D CAD
design software, enhancing the design process by inspiring more innovative ideas among designers as they create their
models. However, our research aims to directly generate 3D CAD models based on prompts provided by users.

Self-refinement Loops: The iterative process used in human problem-solving, such as in CAD design, typi-
cally involves creating an initial model and refining  it based on identified flaws, a method rooted in broader


                                               2problem-solving strategies discussed by [19]. Unlike this human approach, most current language models generate
outputs in a single step, which contrasts with the iterative nature of human task performance [20], [21]. Although
some research, such as [22], has explored post-inference editing, these methods typically do not support multi-step
refinement. Recent advancements include the development of models that integrate iterative feedback mechanisms to
enhance output quality [23]. This model iteratively refines its output by reprocessing its initial output through the
model to obtain and incorporate feedback internally. We aim to incorporate this approach into our architecture, thereby
emulating the way CAD designers iteratively ref

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a comprehensive reference table of ~15 papers on generative CAD / AI-assisted CAD, organized with columns: Reference # | Citation | Category | Summary (3-5 sentences) | Relevance to Python/Code-Based Generative CAD Pipeline for Powder-Dosing Device.

Include the following papers in this order:

1. Wu et al. (2021) - DeepCAD: ICCV 2021. First deep generative model for CAD sequences using Transformer. Dataset of 178,238 models. Category: Landmark Dataset / Foundational Model.

2. Willis et al. (2021) - Fusion 360 Gallery: ACM TOG 2021. 8,625 human design sequences in sketch+extrude language with Fusion 360 Gym environment. Category: Landmark Dataset / Benchmark.

3. Seff et al. (2021) - Vitruvion: arXiv 2021. Autoregressive generative model for parametric CAD sketches with constraints, trained on SketchGraphs (15M sketches from Onshape). Category: Constraint-Aware Sketch Generation / Dataset.

4. Para et al. (2021) - SketchGen: NeurIPS 2021. Generates constrained CAD sketches using Transformer, decomposing into primitives + constraints. Category: Constraint-Aware Generation.

5. Xie & Ju (2025) - Text-to-CadQuery: arXiv 2025. Directly generates CadQuery Python code from text, 170k text-CadQuery pairs, best model 69.3% exact match. Category: Text-to-CAD Code Generation.

6. Wang et al. (2025) - CADFusion: arXiv 2025. LLM-based text-to-CAD with alternating sequential learning and visual feedback via DPO. Category: Text-to-CAD / Visual Feedback.

7. Govindarajan et al. (2025) - CADmium: arXiv 2025. Fine-tunes code-LLMs (Qwen2.5-Coder) on 170k CAD models with GPT-4.1 annotations, JSON-based format, introduces geometric/topological eval metrics. Category: LLM Fine-tuning / Evaluation.

8. Badagabettu et al. (2024) - Query2CAD: arXiv 2024. Zero-shot LLM generates FreeCAD macros with self-refinement loops using BLIP2 feedback. 53.6% first-attempt success, +23.1% with refinement. Category: Agentic Design Loops.

9. Alam & Ahmed (2024/2025) - GenCAD: TMLR 2025. Image-conditioned CAD generation using autoregressive transformers + contrastive learning + latent diffusion. Category: Image-to-CAD / B-rep Generation.

10. Fan et al. (2025) - CADDesigner: arXiv 2025. LLM-powered agent for conceptual CAD with multimodal input, interactive dialogue, knowledge base, ECIP paradigm. Category: Agentic CAD Design.

11. Li et al. (2025) - Seek-CAD: arXiv 2025. Training-free approach using locally deployed DeepSeek-R1 with visual+CoT self-refinement. RAG over CAD code corpus. Category: Training-Free / Agentic Design.

12. Daareyni et al. (2025) - Generative AI meets CAD: Int J Adv Manuf Tech 2025. Open-source Python/Streamlit app integrating GPT with OpenSCAD, 3-layer feedback loop, design-to-manufacturing. Category: Open-Source Pipeline / DfAM.

13. Tsuji et al. (2025) - GenCAD-Self-Repairing: arXiv 2025. Self-repair pipeline for infeasible B-reps using guided diffusion + regression correction. ~10% baseline infeasibility, converts 2/3 to feasible. Category: Feasibility / B-rep Generation.

14. Heidari & Iosifidis (2025) - Geometric Deep Learning for CAD Survey: IEEE Access 2025. Comprehensive survey of GDL for CAD covering datasets (ABC 1M+ models, SketchGraphs 15M sketches, Fusion 360), methods, benchmarks. Category: Survey / Benchmark Overview.

15. Dong et al. (2025) - HistCAD: arXiv 2025. 170,236 executable constraint-aware parametric CAD sequences, Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark with ER/cPCSR/OES metrics. Category: Dataset / Constraint-Aware Evaluation."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Reference # | Citation | Category | Summary (3-5 sentences) | Relevance to Python/Code-Based Generative CAD Pipeline for Powder-Dosing Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Wu et al. (2021) (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024) | Landmark Dataset / Foundational Model | Introduces the first deep generative network that explicitly outputs CAD construction sequences rather than discrete shape representations. Uses a Transformer-based autoencoder to encode and decode parameterized commands. Released a massive dataset of 178,238 CAD models paired with command sequences. | Provides the foundational sequence-based representation and dataset scale needed to train neural CAD engines. |
| 2 | Willis et al. (2021) (pqac-00000011) | Landmark Dataset / Benchmark | Introduces a large dataset of 8,625 human-designed CAD programs encoded as sketch-and-extrude sequences. Provides the Fusion 360 Gym execution environment that frames sequential construction as a Markov decision process. Enables benchmarking CAD reconstruction from target geometry using neurally-guided search. | Useful for understanding human design patterns and structuring reinforcement learning environments for code generation. |
| 3 | Seff et al. (2021) (pqac-00000028) | Constraint-Aware Sketch Generation / Dataset | Presents an autoregressive generative model for parametric CAD sketches that outputs both geometric primitives and explicit constraints. Trained on the SketchGraphs dataset comprising millions of real-world sketches. Uses self-attention to propagate state and outputs graphs compatible with standard constraint solvers. | Crucial for ensuring that generated 2D profiles for device components maintain valid geometric constraints before being extruded into 3D. |
| 4 | Para et al. (2021) (pqac-00000027, pqac-00000029) | Constraint-Aware Generation | A generative framework that outputs constrained sketches by decomposing the generation into primitives and their respective constraints. Uses a transformer architecture capturing long-range dependencies over a sequential token representation. Employs continuous parameter quantization followed by traditional constraint solvers to refine outputs. | Directly applicable for robustly generating and constraining 2D base profiles for mechanical parts. |
| 5 | Xie & Ju (2025) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) | Text-to-CAD Code Generation | Proposes generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language prompts using LLMs. Releases a massive dataset of 170k text-to-CadQuery pairs and fine-tunes models up to 7B parameters. Achieves a 69.3% exact match rate, demonstrating that scaling pretrained LLMs yields highly executable code. | Highly relevant as it directly outputs pure Python (CadQuery) scripts, perfectly aligning with a programmatic open-source CAD pipeline. |
| 6 | Wang et al. (2025) (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000032) | Text-to-CAD / Visual Feedback | An LLM-based text-to-CAD framework that integrates visual feedback to guide generation. Alternates between sequential learning on ground-truth parametric sequences and visual feedback stages using preference optimization (DPO). Rewards generated sequences that render into visually preferred models, overcoming the many-to-one rendering ambiguity. | Highlights how visual validation loops can improve the physical accuracy and visual fidelity of generated device components. |
| 7 | Govindarajan et al. (2025) (pqac-00000036) | LLM Fine-tuning / Evaluation | Reformulates CAD generation as a pure text-to-text task by fine-tuning code-LLMs (like Qwen2.5-Coder) on 170k models annotated with GPT-4.1. Uses a minimal JSON-based format to represent sequential CAD designs. Introduces advanced geometric and topological metrics (sphericity, mean curvature, Euler characteristic) to evaluate generated structures. | Demonstrates effective fine-tuning strategies for code-LLMs and provides rigorous metrics to validate generated mechanical parts. |
| 8 | Badagabettu et al. (2024) (pqac-00000038) | Agentic Design Loops | A zero-shot LLM framework that generates Python macros for FreeCAD based on natural language queries. Integrates a self-refinement loop that uses BLIP2 visual question-answering feedback to iterate on designs. Achieves a 53.6% success rate on the first attempt, improving by 23.1% after refinement iterations. | Directly matches the goal of creating a Python-based open-source CAD generator, providing a blueprint for agentic refinement loops. |
| 9 | Alam & Ahmed (2024) (pqac-00000035) | Image-to-CAD / B-rep Generation | An image-conditioned generative model that outputs parametric CAD command sequences for editable 3D shapes. Combines autoregressive transformers, a contrastive learning framework, and latent diffusion priors. Outperforms existing methods on unconditional and conditional CAD model generation tasks. | Demonstrates how multimodal inputs (like sketches or images of a powder device) can be translated into parametric code sequences. |
| 10 | Fan et al. (2025) (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000020) | Agentic CAD Design | An LLM-powered agent for conceptual CAD design that processes multimodal inputs and engages in interactive dialogue. Uses an Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) to decouple complex operations and stores cases in a knowledge base for improved generation. Integrates iterative visual feedback to refine the fidelity of the generated models. | Provides an architecture for an interactive, agentic design loop that can iteratively refine mechanical component code. |
| 11 | Li et al. (2025) (pqac-00000034) | Training-Free / Agentic Design | A training-free generative CAD approach using the locally deployed DeepSeek-R1 open-source model. Leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a CAD code corpus and a step-wise visual/Chain-of-Thought feedback loop to self-refine outputs. Introduces a new dataset organized around a Sketch-SketchFeature-Refinement (SSR) paradigm. | Highly relevant for building cost-effective, locally-hosted generative pipelines using open-source reasoning models. |
| 12 | Daareyni et al. (2025) (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000033) | Open-Source Pipeline / DfAM | Presents an open-source Python/Streamlit application linking GPT-4 with OpenSCAD for text/sketch-to-CAD workflows. Implements a three-layer closed-loop feedback mechanism involving syntax checks, LLM self-evaluation, and user feedback. Evaluates LLM-assisted CAD modeling for direct design-to-manufacturing applications. | Provides a practical, open-source architectural blueprint for translating AI outputs into manufacturable scripts. |
| 13 | Tsuji et al. (2025) (pqac-00000018) | Feasibility / B-rep Generation | Addresses the limitation that generative CAD models often produce infeasible boundary representations (~10% failure rate). Proposes a framework utilizing guided diffusion denoising and regression-based self-repair to correct faulty command sequences. Converts two-thirds of previously infeasible designs into viable, geometrically accurate models. | Critical for ensuring that AI-generated scripts for physical devices compile into manufacturable and watertight solids. |
| 14 | Heidari & Iosifidis (2025) (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000037) | Survey / Benchmark Overview | A comprehensive review of geometric deep learning applied to CAD. Details major benchmark datasets such as the ABC dataset (1M+ B-rep models), SketchGraphs (15M constraint graphs), and Fusion 360 Gallery. Highlights the shift from manual heuristics to automated feature representation and constraint synthesis. | Acts as a central reference point for understanding the landscape of datasets and benchmarks available for training CAD generators. |
| 15 | Dong et al. (2025) (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000031) | Dataset / Constraint-Aware Evaluation | Introduces a massive dataset of 170,236 executable constraint-aware parametric CAD sequences to bridge the gap between pure geometry and editability. Flattens sketch geometry into explicit geometric and dimensional constraint graphs. Presents a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark to evaluate how well models preserve design intent during edits. | Essential for ensuring that generated programmatic code includes valid constraints, keeping the open-source device editable and robust. |


*Table: A structured summary of key academic papers from 2021-2025 focusing on generative CAD, constraint-aware sketching, and LLM-driven code generation, mapped to their relevance for programmatic hardware design.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00]

Context IDs that may be cited: [pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002, pqac-00000003, pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006, pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000016, pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000035, pqac-00000036, pqac-00000037, pqac-00000038]

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant

        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Find recent (2021-2025) peer-reviewed and arXiv academic publications on generative CAD, AI-assisted CAD, LLM-based CAD code generation, parametric/programmatic CAD, and learning-based shape generation for engineering design. Cover capabilities (text-to-CAD, sketch-to-CAD, constraint-aware generation, design-for-AM, B-rep generation, CSG / feature-tree generation, agentic design loops) and limitations (manufacturability, parametric editability, accuracy, evaluation benchmarks, hallucination of features, lack of standard datasets). For each paper give: full citation, 3-5 sentence summary, and relevance to building a Python/code-based generative CAD pipeline for an open-source powder-dosing device. Include landmark datasets/benchmarks (DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery, ABC dataset, SketchGraphs, Text2CAD). Aim for ~10-15 references.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models

    author = "Daareyni, Amirmohammad and Martikkala, Antti and Mokhtarian, Hossein and Ituarte, Iñigo Flores",
    title = "Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models",
    year = "2025",

    month = "Jun",

Relevant Snippet:
… a text-to-CAD … this LLM-driven approach to CAD modeling, we aim to lay the groundwork for broader, more accessible design-to-manufacturing workflows that leverage state-of-the-art AI …


---

## 2. Text-to-CadQuery: A New Paradigm for CAD Generation with Scalable Large Model Capabilities
Valid Text Names:
'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 3-4', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 4-6', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 6-8', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 10-12', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15', 'xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 15-18'

BibTex:
@article{xie2505texttocadqueryanew,
    author = "Xie, Haoyang and Ju, Feng",
    title = "Text-to-CadQuery: A New Paradigm for CAD Generation with Scalable Large Model Capabilities",
    year = "2505",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.06507",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.06507"
}


Abstract:
Computer-aided design (CAD) is fundamental to modern engineering and manufacturing, but creating CAD models still requires expert knowledge and specialized software. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) open up the possibility of generative CAD, where natural language is directly translated into parametric 3D models. However, most existing methods generate task-specific command sequences that pretrained models cannot directly handle. These sequences must be converted into CAD representations such as CAD vectors before a 3D model can be produced, which requires training models from scratch and adds unnecessary complexity. To tackle this issue, we propose generating CadQuery code directly from text, leveraging the strengths of pretrained LLMs to produce 3D models without intermediate representations, using this Python-based scripting language. Since LLMs already excel at Python generation and spatial reasoning, fine-tuning them on Text-to-CadQuery data proves highly effective. Given that these capabilities typically improve with scale, we hypothesize that larger models will perform better after fine-tuning. To enable this, we augment the Text2CAD dataset with 170,000 CadQuery annotations. We fine-tune six open-source LLMs of varying sizes and observe consistent improvements. Our best model achieves a top-1 exact match of 69.3%, up from 58.8%, and reduces Chamfer Distance by 48.6%. Project page: https://github.com/Text-to-CadQuery/Text-to-CadQuery.

Relevant Snippet:
… [1] have introduced early Text-to-CAD tools, reflecting the … This work focuses on the output format for generative CAD, … a key limitation of current LLM-based CAD generation: while basic …


---

## 5. CADmium: Fine-Tuning Code Language Models for Text-Driven Sequential CAD Design
Valid Text Names:

    year = "2025",

    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2507.09792",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2507.09792"
}


Abstract:
Computer-aided design (CAD) is the digital construction of 2D and 3D objects, and is central to a wide range of engineering and manufacturing applications like automobile and aviation. Despite its importance, CAD modeling remains largely a time-intensive, manual task. Recent works have attempted to automate this process with small transformer-based models and handcrafted CAD sequence representations. However, there has been little effort to leverage the potential of large language models (LLMs) for sequential CAD design. In this work, we introduce a new large-scale dataset of more than 170k CAD models annotated with high-quality, human-like descriptions generated with our pipeline based on GPT-4.1. Using this dataset, we fine-tune powerful code-LLMs to generate CAD sequences represented in a JSON-based format from natural language descriptions, demonstrating the viability and effectiveness of this approach for text-conditioned CAD generation. Because simple metrics often fail to reflect the quality of generated objects, we introduce geometric and topological metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic to provide richer structural insights. Our experiments and ablation studies on both synthetic and human-annotated data demonstrate that CADmium is able to automate CAD design, drastically speeding up the design of new objects. The dataset, code, and fine-tuned models are available online.

Abstract:
Designing complex computer-aided design (CAD) models is often time-consuming due to challenges such as computational inefficiency and the difficulty of generating precise models. We propose a novel language-guided framework for industrial design automation to address these issues, integrating large language models (LLMs) with computer-automated design (CAutoD).Through this framework, CAD models are automatically generated from parameters and appearance descriptions, supporting the automation of design tasks during the detailed CAD design phase. Our approach introduces three key innovations: (1) a semi-automated data annotation pipeline that leverages LLMs and vision-language large models (VLLMs) to generate high-quality parameters and appearance descriptions; (2) a Transformer-based CAD generator (TCADGen) that predicts modeling sequences via dual-channel feature aggregation; (3) an enhanced CAD modeling generation model, called CADLLM, that is designed to refine the generated sequences by incorporating the confidence scores from TCADGen. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms traditional methods in both accuracy and efficiency, providing a powerful tool for automating industrial workflows and generating complex CAD models from textual prompts. The code is available at https://jianxliao.github.io/cadllm-page/

## 8. Text-to-CAD Generation Through Infusing Visual Feedback in Large Language Models
Valid Text Names:
'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 2-3', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 3-4', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 4-5', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 6-8', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 8-9', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 9-10', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 10-12', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 12-12', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 13-15', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 13-13', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 15-15', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 16-18', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 16-16', 'wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 18-19'

    author = "Wang, Ruiyu and Yuan, Yu and Sun, Shizhao and Bian, Jiang",
    title = "Text-to-CAD Generation Through Infusing Visual Feedback in Large Language Models",
    year = "2501",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2501.19054",
    month = "Jan",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2501.19054",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2501.19054"
}


Abstract:
Creating Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models requires significant expertise and effort. Text-to-CAD, which converts textual descriptions into CAD parametric sequences, is crucial in streamlining this process. Recent studies have utilized ground-truth parametric sequences, known as sequential signals, as supervision to achieve this goal. However, CAD models are inherently multimodal, comprising parametric sequences and corresponding rendered visual objects. Besides,the rendering process from parametric sequences to visual objects is many-to-one. Therefore, both sequential and visual signals are critical for effective training. In this work, we introduce CADFusion, a framework that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) as the backbone and alternates between two training stages: the sequential learning (SL) stage and the visual feedback (VF) stage. In the SL stage, we train LLMs using ground-truth parametric sequences, enabling the generation of logically coherent parametric sequences. In the VF stage, we reward parametric sequences that render into visually preferred objects and penalize those that do not, allowing LLMs to learn how rendered visual objects are perceived and evaluated. These two stages alternate throughout the training, ensuring balanced learning and preserving benefits of both signals. Experiments demonstrate that CADFusion significantly improves performance, both qualitatively and quantitatively.

## 1. Integrating deep learning into CAD/CAE system: generative design and evaluation of 3D conceptual wheel
Valid Text Names:

}


Abstract:
Engineering design research integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into computer-aided design (CAD) and computer-aided engineering (CAE) is actively being conducted. This study proposes a deep learning-based CAD/CAE framework in the conceptual design phase that automatically generates 3D CAD designs and evaluates their engineering performance. The proposed framework comprises seven stages: (1) 2D generative design, (2) dimensionality reduction, (3) design of experiment in latent space, (4) CAD automation, (5) CAE automation, (6) transfer learning, and (7) visualization and analysis. The proposed framework is demonstrated through a road wheel design case study and indicates that AI can be practically incorporated into an end-use product design project. Engineers and industrial designers can jointly review a large number of generated 3D CAD models by using this framework along with the engineering performance results estimated by AI and find conceptual design candidates for the subsequent detailed design stage.

Abstract:
Geometric Deep Learning techniques have become a transformative force in the field of Computer-Aided Design (CAD), and have the potential to revolutionize how designers and engineers approach and enhance the design process. By harnessing the power of machine learning-based methods, CAD designers can optimize their workflows, save time and effort while making better informed decisions, and create designs that are both innovative and practical. The ability to process the CAD designs represented by geometric data and to analyze their encoded features enables the identification of similarities among diverse CAD models, the proposition of alternative designs and enhancements, and even the generation of novel design alternatives. This survey offers a comprehensive overview of learning-based methods in computer-aided design across various categories, including similarity analysis and retrieval, 2D and 3D CAD model synthesis, and CAD generation from point clouds, and single/multi-view images. Additionally, it provides a complete list of benchmark datasets and their characteristics, along with open-source codes that have propelled research in this domain. The final discussion delves into the challenges prevalent in this field, followed by potential future research directions in this rapidly evolving field.

## 5. GenCAD-Self-Repairing: Feasibility Enhancement for 3D CAD Generation
Valid Text Names:

    author = "Tsuji, Chikaha and Medina, Enrique Flores and Gupta, Harshit and Alam, Md Ferdous",
    title = "GenCAD-Self-Repairing: Feasibility Enhancement for 3D CAD Generation",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2505.23287",
    month = "May",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.23287"
}


Abstract:
With the advancement of generative AI, research on its application to 3D model generation has gained traction, particularly in automating the creation of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) files from images. GenCAD is a notable model in this domain, leveraging an autoregressive transformer-based architecture with a contrastive learning framework to generate CAD programs. However, a major limitation of GenCAD is its inability to consistently produce feasible boundary representations (B-reps), with approximately 10% of generated designs being infeasible. To address this, we propose GenCAD-Self-Repairing, a framework that enhances the feasibility of generative CAD models through diffusion guidance and a self-repairing pipeline. This framework integrates a guided diffusion denoising process in the latent space and a regression-based correction mechanism to refine infeasible CAD command sequences while preserving geometric accuracy. Our approach successfully converted two-thirds of infeasible designs in the baseline method into feasible ones, significantly improving the feasibility rate while simultaneously maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models. By significantly improving the feasibility rate of generating CAD models, our approach helps expand the availability of high-quality training data and enhances the applicability of AI-driven CAD generation in manufacturing, architecture, and product design.

Abstract:
The creation of manufacturable and editable 3D shapes through Computer-Aided Design (CAD) remains a highly manual and time-consuming task, hampered by the complex topology of boundary representations of 3D solids and unintuitive design tools. While most work in the 3D shape generation literature focuses on representations like meshes, voxels, or point clouds, practical engineering applications demand the modifiability and manufacturability of CAD models and the ability for multi-modal conditional CAD model generation. This paper introduces GenCAD, a generative model that employs autoregressive transformers with a contrastive learning framework and latent diffusion models to transform image inputs into parametric CAD command sequences, resulting in editable 3D shape representations. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that GenCAD significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of the unconditional and conditional generations of CAD models. Additionally, the contrastive learning framework of GenCAD facilitates the retrieval of CAD models using image queries from large CAD databases, which is a critical challenge within the CAD community. Our results provide a significant step forward in highlighting the potential of generative models to expedite the entire design-to-production pipeline and seamlessly integrate different design modalities.

## 1. DeepCAD: A Deep Generative Network for Computer-Aided Design Models

Abstract:
Deep generative models of 3D shapes have received a great deal of research interest. Yet, almost all of them generate discrete shape representations, such as voxels, point clouds, and polygon meshes. We present the first 3D generative model for a drastically different shape representation— describing a shape as a sequence of computer-aided design (CAD) operations. Unlike meshes and point clouds, CAD models encode the user creation process of 3D shapes, widely used in numerous industrial and engineering design tasks. However, the sequential and irregular structure of CAD operations poses significant challenges for existing 3D generative models. Drawing an analogy between CAD operations and natural language, we propose a CAD generative network based on the Transformer. We demonstrate the performance of our model for both shape autoencoding and random shape generation. To train our network, we create a new CAD dataset consisting of 178,238 models and their CAD construction sequences. We have made this dataset publicly available to promote future research on this topic.

Abstract:
Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved significant success, prompting increased interest in expanding their generative capabilities beyond general text into domain-specific areas. This study investigates the generation of parametric sequences for computer-aided design (CAD) models using LLMs. This endeavor represents an initial step towards creating parametric 3D shapes with LLMs, as CAD model parameters directly correlate with shapes in three-dimensional space. Despite the formidable generative capacities of LLMs, this task remains challenging, as these models neither encounter parametric sequences during their pretraining phase nor possess direct awareness of 3D structures. To address this, we present CAD-Llama, a framework designed to enhance pretrained LLMs for generating parametric 3D CAD models. Specifically, we develop a hierarchical annotation pipeline and a code-like format to translate parametric 3D CAD command sequences into Structured Parametric CAD Code (SPCC), incorporating hierarchical semantic descriptions. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive pretraining approach utilizing SPCC, followed by an instruction tuning process aligned with CAD-specific guidelines. This methodology aims to equip LLMs with the spatial knowledge inherent in parametric sequences. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms prior autoregressive methods and existing LLM baselines.

## 3. CADCrafter: Generating Computer-Aided Design Models from Unconstrained Images
Valid Text Names:
'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 1-2', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 2-3', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 3-4', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 4-6', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 6-7', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 7-8', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 8-9', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 9-11', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 11-12', 'chen2025cadcraftergeneratingcomputeraided pages 12-13'

    title = "CADCrafter: Generating Computer-Aided Design Models from Unconstrained Images",

}


Abstract:
Creating CAD digital twins from the physical world is crucial for manufacturing, design, and simulation. However, current methods typically rely on costly 3D scanning with labor-intensive post-processing. To provide a user-friendly design process, we explore the problem of reverse engineering from unconstrained real-world CAD images that can be easily captured by users of all experiences. However, the scarcity of real-world CAD data poses challenges in directly training such models. To tackle these challenges, we propose CADCrafter, an image-to-parametric CAD model generation framework that trains solely on synthetic textureless CAD data while testing on real-world images. To bridge the significant representation disparity between images and parametric CAD models, we introduce a geometry encoder to accurately capture diverse geometric features. Moreover, the texture-invariant properties of the geometric features can also facilitate the generalization to real-world scenarios. Since compiling CAD parameter sequences into explicit CAD models is a non-differentiable process, the network training inherently lacks explicit geometric supervision. To impose geometric validity constraints, we employ direct preference optimization (DPO) to fine-tune our model with the automatic code checker feedback on CAD sequence quality. Furthermore, we collected a real-world dataset, comprised of multi-view images and corresponding CAD command sequence pairs, to evaluate our method. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach can robustly handle real unconstrained CAD images, and even generalize to unseen general objects.

Abstract:
This paper presents a novel generative model for Computer Aided Design (CAD) that 1) represents high-level design concepts of a CAD model as a three-level hierarchical tree of neural codes, from global part arrangement down to local curve geometry; and 2) controls the generation or completion of CAD models by specifying the target design using a code tree. Concretely, a novel variant of a vector quantized VAE with"masked skip connection"extracts design variations as neural codebooks at three levels. Two-stage cascaded auto-regressive transformers learn to generate code trees from incomplete CAD models and then complete CAD models following the intended design. Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance on conventional tasks such as random generation while enabling novel interaction capabilities on conditional generation tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/samxuxiang/hnc-cad.

Abstract:
In this paper, we present CAD2Program, a new method for reconstructing 3D parametric models from 2D CAD drawings. Our proposed method is inspired by recent successes in vision-language models (VLMs), and departs from traditional methods which rely on task-specific data representations and/or algorithms. Specifically, on the input side, we simply treat the 2D CAD drawing as a raster image, regardless of its original format, and encode the image with a standard ViT model. We show that such an encoding scheme achieves competitive performance against existing methods that operate on vector-graphics inputs, while imposing substantially fewer restrictions on the 2D drawings. On the output side, our method auto-regressively predicts a general-purpose language describing 3D parametric models in text form. Compared to other sequence modeling methods for CAD which use domain-specific sequence representations with fixed-size slots, our text-based representation is more flexible, and can be easily extended to arbitrary geometric entities and semantic or functional properties. Experimental results on a large-scale dataset of cabinet models demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.

Abstract:
<jats:p>Computer-Aided Design (CAD) systems are foundational to modern engineering, enabling the creation of precise digital models. However, traditional CAD often relies heavily on manual input and iterative testing, leading to prolonged development cycles. The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI), specifically Neural Networks, presents a transformative opportunity to overcome these limitations. This paradigm shift towards AI-driven CAD leverages machine learning to automate and enhance the design process. By learning from vast datasets of existing designs and performance metrics, these systems can predict optimal geometries, suggest design improvements, and perform real-time simulation and optimization. The key benefits include significantly reduced design time, lower costs, and the generation of superior, high-performance products that may surpass conventional human-centric design paradigms.</jats:p>

## 8. Seek-CAD: A Self-refined Generative Modeling for 3D Parametric CAD Using Local Inference via DeepSeek

    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.17702"
}


Abstract:
The advent of Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling will significantly transform the design of industrial products. The recent research endeavor has extended into the realm of Large Language Models (LLMs). In contrast to fine-tuning methods, training-free approaches typically utilize the advanced closed-source LLMs, thereby offering enhanced flexibility and efficiency in the development of AI agents for generating CAD parametric models. However, the substantial cost and limitations of local deployment of the top-tier closed-source LLMs pose challenges in practical applications. The Seek-CAD is the pioneer exploration of locally deployed open-source inference LLM DeepSeek-R1 for CAD parametric model generation with a training-free methodology. This study is the first investigation to incorporate both visual and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) feedback within the self-refinement mechanism for generating CAD models. Specifically, the initial generated parametric CAD model is rendered into a sequence of step-wise perspective images, which are subsequently processed by a Vision Language Model (VLM) alongside the corresponding CoTs derived from DeepSeek-R1 to assess the CAD model generation. Then, the feedback is utilized by DeepSeek-R1 to refine the initial generated model for the next round of generation. Moreover, we present an innovative 3D CAD model dataset structured around the SSR (Sketch, Sketch-based feature, and Refinements) triple design paradigm. This dataset encompasses a wide range of CAD commands, thereby aligning effectively with industrial application requirements and proving suitable for the generation of LLMs. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of Seek-CAD under various metrics.

## 11. Neural surrogate-driven modelling, optimisation, and generation of engineering designs: A concise review

Abstract:
Abstract. Synergies between neural networks and traditional surrogate modelling techniques have emerged as the forefront of data-driven engineering. Neural network-based surrogate models, trained on carefully selected experimental data or high-fidelity simulations, can predict behaviours of complex systems with remarkable speed and accuracy. This review examines the current state and recent developments in neural surrogate technologies, highlighting their expanding roles in engineering design optimisation and generation. It also covers various feature engineering methods for representing 3D geometries, the principles of neural surrogate modelling, and the potential of emerging AI-driven design tools. While feature engineering remains a challenge, especially in parameterising complex designs for machine learning, recent advancements in code/language-based representations offer promising solutions for digitalising various design scenarios. Moreover, the emergence of AI-driven design tools, including text-to-CAD models powered by large language models, enables engineers to rapidly generate and evaluate innovative design concepts. Neural surrogate modelling has the potential to transform engineering workflows. Continued research into geometric feature engineering, along with the integration of AI-driven design tools, will speed up the use of neural surrogate models in engineering designs.

    issue = "5",
    issn = "2374-3468"
}


Abstract:
Computer-Aided Design (CAD) generative modeling has a strong and long-term application in the industry. Recently, the parametric CAD sequence as the design logic of an object has been widely mined by sequence models. However, the industrial CAD models, especially in component objects, are fine-grained and complex, requiring a longer parametric CAD sequence to define. To address the problem, we introduce Mamba-CAD, a self-supervised generative modeling for complex CAD models in the industry, which can model on a longer parametric CAD sequence. Specifically, we first design an encoder-decoder framework based on a Mamba architecture and pair it with a CAD reconstruction task for pre-training to model the latent representation of CAD models; and then we utilize the learned representation to guide a generative adversarial network to produce the fake representation of CAD models, which would be finally recovered into parametric CAD sequences via the decoder of Mamba-CAD. To train Mamba-CAD, we further create a new dataset consisting of 77,078 CAD models with longer parametric CAD sequences. Comprehensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of our model under various evaluation metrics, especially in the generation length of valid parametric CAD sequences.

    publisher = "arXiv"

Abstract:
The generation of industrial Computer-Aided Design (CAD) models from user requests and specifications is crucial to enhancing efficiency in modern manufacturing. Traditional methods of CAD generation rely heavily on manual inputs and struggle with complex or non-standard designs, making them less suited for dynamic industrial needs. To overcome these challenges, we introduce Text2CAD, a novel framework that employs stable diffusion models tailored to automate the generation process and efficiently bridge the gap between user specifications in text and functional CAD models. This approach directly translates the user's textural descriptions into detailed isometric images, which are then precisely converted into orthographic views, e.g., top, front, and side, providing sufficient information to reconstruct 3D CAD models. This process not only streamlines the creation of CAD models from textual descriptions but also ensures that the resulting models uphold physical and dimensional consistency essential for practical engineering applications. Our experimental results show that Text2CAD effectively generates technical drawings that are accurately translated into high-quality 3D CAD models, showing substantial potential to revolutionize CAD automation in response to user demands.

## 3. Query2CAD: Generating CAD models using natural language queries

    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2406.00144",
    publisher = "arXiv"
