Flattens sketch geometry into explicit geometric and dimensional constraint graphs. Presents a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark to evaluate how well models preserve design intent during edits. | Essential for ensuring that generated programmatic code includes valid constraints, keeping the open-source device editable and robust. |


*Table: A structured summary of key academic papers from 2021-2025 focusing on generative CAD, constraint-aware sketching, and LLM-driven code generation, mapped to their relevance for programmatic hardware design.*

### 2. Foundational Datasets and Benchmarks

**DeepCAD** (Wu et al., ICCV 2021) established the first large-scale dataset of 178,238 CAD models represented as parameterized command sequences (sketch + extrude operations). The Transformer-based autoencoder treats CAD operations analogously to natural language tokens, with continuous parameters quantized into 256 levels to convert regression into classification (wu2021deepcadadeep pages 1-2, wu2021deepcadadeep pages 2-3, wu2021deepcadadeep pages 3-4). Evaluation metrics introduced include Chamfer Distance (CD), Command Accuracy (~99.3%), Parameter Accuracy (~97.3%), and Invalid Ratio (2.7–3.5%) (lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19). DeepCAD remains the most widely used training corpus for generative CAD research.

**Fusion 360 Gallery** (Willis et al., ACM TOG 2021) provides 8,625 human-designed CAD programs in a compact sketch-and-extrude language, alongside the Fusion 360 Gym interactive environment that exposes construction as a Markov decision process (willis2021fusion360gallery pages 1-2). The dataset also includes an assembly subset with 8,251 assemblies and 154,468 parts with joint annotations (lin2025asurveyon pages 13-14, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 6-7). It remains the primary benchmark for CAD reconstruction from target geometry.

**SketchGraphs** consists of 15 million real-world parametric 2D sketches from Onshape, each represented as a geometric constraint graph with nodes (primitives) and edges (constraints such as coincidence, tangency, and parallelism) (heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 5-6, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 12-13). An open-source Python processing pipeline is provided. The **ABC Dataset** contributes over 1 million high-quality B-rep CAD models with precisely parameterized curves and surfaces, though it lacks construction histories (lin2025asurveyon pages 11-13, lin2025asurveyon pages 13-14, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 5-6).

### 3. Text-to-CAD and LLM-Based Code Generation

The most directly relevant paradigm for a Python-based pipeline is **Text-to-CadQuery** (Xie & Ju, 2025), which generates executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language descriptions. The authors augmented the DeepCAD/Text2CAD data with 170,000 CadQuery annotations and fine-tuned six LLMs (124M–7B parameters), achieving a top-1 exact match of 69.3% and a 48.6% reduction in Chamfer Distance (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3). A fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini achieved a 0% invalid rate, demonstrating that larger models can produce fully executable code (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10). This approach leverages the fact that foundation LLMs already excel at Python generation and spatial reasoning (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3).

**CADmium** (Govindarajan et al., 2025) reformulates CAD generation as a text-to-text task by fine-tuning code-LLMs such as Qwen2.5-Coder using LoRA on 170k+ models annotated with GPT-4.1-generated descriptions. It uses a JSON-based format for CAD sequences and introduces geometric and topological evaluation metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic (govindarajan2025cadmiumfinetuningcode pages 1-2).

**Query2CAD** (Badagabettu et al., 2024) demonstrates a zero-shot approach where GPT-4 Turbo generates Python macros for FreeCAD without supervised training. It implements self-refinement loops using BLIP2 visual question-answering feedback, achieving 53.6% success on the first attempt and improving by 23.1% through iterative refinement (badagabettu2024query2cadgeneratingcad pages 1-3).

**Daareyni et al. (2025)** present GAD, an open-source Python/Streamlit application integrating GPT-4 with OpenSCAD for multimodal text/sketch/voice-to-CAD workflows. The system implements a three-layer closed-loop feedback mechanism (syntax checking, LLM self-evaluation, and user feedback) and supports direct export to STL and G-code for manufacturing (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 2-4).

### 4. Visual Feedback and Agentic Design Loops

**CADFusion** (Wang et al., 2025) uses LLMs as the backbone and alternates between sequential learning on ground-truth parametric sequences and a visual feedback stage that rewards sequences rendering into visually preferred objects using DPO (direct preference optimization). This addresses the many-to-one rendering ambiguity inherent in CAD, where multiple parametric sequences can produce the same visual object (wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 2-3, wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 3-4).

**CADDesigner** (Fan et al., 2025) is an LLM-powered agent for conceptual CAD that accepts multimodal input (text + sketches), engages in interactive dialogue for requirement analysis, and supports a broad range of operations including extrusion, revolution, fillet/chamfer, sweeping, and lofting. It uses an Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) to decouple CAD operations and stores cases in a knowledge base for improved code generation (fan2025caddesignerconceptualdesign pages 1-2, fan2025caddesignerconceptualdesign pages 8-9).

**Seek-CAD** (Li et al., 2025) is a training-free approach using the locally deployed open-source DeepSeek-R1 model with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a CAD code corpus. It employs step-wise visual feedback combined with Chain-of-Thought reasoning from a Vision-Language Model to iteratively self-refine generated parametric CAD code (li2505seekcadaselfrefined pages 1-2).

### 5. Constraint-Aware Sketch and Shape Generation

**SketchGen** (Para et al., 2021) addresses constrained sketch generation by decomposing the task into primitive generation and conditional constraint generation, p(S) = p(C|P)p(P), using two autoregressive Transformer networks. Continuous parameters are aggressively quantized during generation, then refined by a traditional constraint solver to correct discretization errors (para2021sketchgengeneratingconstrained pages 1-3, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 14-15, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 13-14).

**Vitruvion** (Seff et al., 2021) similarly autoregressively generates both primitives and explicit constraints trained on SketchGraphs, but additionally supports conditioning on hand-drawn sketches for reverse engineering from noisy inputs. Its outputs are constraint-graph representations compatible with standard CAD constraint solvers, enabling import, solving, and subsequent editable modifications (seff2021vitruvionagenerative pages 1-3).

**HistCAD** (Dong et al., 2025) introduces 170,236 executable constraint-aware parametric CAD sequences in a CAD-backend-independent intermediate language. It explicitly encodes 19 constraint types and defines a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark with metrics for Edit Reachability (ER), Conditional Preserved Constraint Satisfaction Rate (cPCSR), and Overall Editable Success (OES), demonstrating that explicit constraints are essential for preserving design intent after parameter edits (dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 2-3, dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 1-2).

### 6. Image-to-CAD and B-rep Generation

**GenCAD** (Alam & Ahmed, 2024/2025) combines autoregressive transformers, contrastive learning, and latent diffusion priors to generate parametric CAD command sequences conditioned on images. It significantly outperforms prior methods on both unconditional and conditional generation tasks while also enabling image-based retrieval from large CAD databases (alam2409gencadimageconditionedcomputeraided pages 1-3).

**GenCAD-Self-Repairing** (Tsuji et al., 2025) addresses the critical limitation that approximately 10% of generated B-rep designs are infeasible. It proposes a guided diffusion denoising process in latent space combined with regression-based correction to repair infeasible command sequences, converting two-thirds of infeasible designs to feasible ones while maintaining geometric accuracy (tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 1-2).

### 7. Survey Coverage

**Heidari & Iosifidis (2025)** provide a comprehensive survey of geometric deep learning for CAD, covering similarity analysis, retrieval, 2D/3D model synthesis, and generation from point clouds and images. The survey catalogs all major benchmark datasets—ABC (1M+ models), SketchGraphs (15M sketches), Fusion 360 Gallery—and notes that public CAD databases are predominantly unlabeled, posing obstacles for supervised learning (heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 5-6, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 6-7, heidari2025geometricdeeplearning pages 1-2).

### 8. Key Capabilities

Current systems demonstrate several capabilities relevant to building a generative CAD pipeline:

- **Text-to-CAD code generation**: Direct generation of executable Python (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, FreeCAD macros) from natural language, with best models achieving 69.3% exact match and 0% invalid rate at scale (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 8-10, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3).
- **Constraint-aware generation**: Explicit constraint graphs for sketch primitives enabling valid geometric relationships (parallelism, tangency, concentricity) that are essential for manufacturable parts (para2021sketchgengeneratingconstrained pages 1-3, seff2021vitruvionagenerative pages 1-3, dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 2-3).
- **Agentic design loops**: Self-refinement via visual feedback (VLM rendering + VQA scoring), Chain-of-Thought reasoning, and human-in-the-loop mechanisms that iteratively improve generated designs (kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29, wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 3-4, li2505seekcadaselfrefined pages 1-2, badagabettu2024query2cadgeneratingcad pages 1-3).
- **Design-to-manufacturing pipelines**: Open-source frameworks supporting direct export to STL, G-code, and connections to 3D printers (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 2-4).

### 9. Key Limitations

Several persistent limitations must be addressed for practical deployment:

- **Geometric hallucination**: LLMs may incorrectly generate circular profiles for hexagonal nuts, miss flange boreholes, or produce incorrect spring helices due to limited geometric reasoning (kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29).
- **Manufacturability gaps**: Generated models often lack validation for wall thickness, overhang angles, support structures, and other additive manufacturing constraints. Virtually approved designs can fail physically (kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 21-25, fan2025caddesignerconceptualdesign pages 8-9).
- **Parametric editability**: Training solely on parametric sequences may bias models toward training-set patterns, overlooking valid alternative constructions. Without explicit constraints, edits cause geometric distortion and loss of perpendicularity or tangency (wang2501texttocadgenerationthrough pages 2-3, dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 2-3).
- **B-rep feasibility**: Approximately 10% of generated B-reps are infeasible, and the likelihood of invalid models increases with longer, more complex command sequences (tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 1-2, kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29).
- **Evaluation challenges**: Standard metrics (Chamfer Distance, IoU) often fail to capture structural or topological quality. Newer metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic are being introduced (govindarajan2025cadmiumfinetuningcode pages 1-2, lin2025asurveyon pages 18-19).
- **Dataset limitations**: Existing datasets are biased toward simple shapes, exclude certain CAD features (fillets, chamfers), and lack natural language annotations or manufacturing metadata (kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29, liao2025automatedcadmodeling pages 1-2).

### 10. Implications for a Python/Code-Based Generative CAD Pipeline for an Open-Source Powder-Dosing Device

For building a generative CAD pipeline targeting a powder-dosing device, several architectural recommendations emerge from this literature:

1. **Output representation**: CadQuery Python code is the most promising target, as it is a pure-Python, open-source API that does not require proprietary CAD software, and pretrained LLMs already demonstrate strong Python generation capabilities (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 1-3).
2. **Self-refinement**: Implementing an agentic loop with visual feedback (rendering the generated model and using a VLM to verify geometric fidelity) is critical, as first-attempt success rates remain modest (53–69%) but improve substantially with iteration (badagabettu2024query2cadgeneratingcad pages 1-3, kilpelainen2025utilizinggenerativeai pages 25-29).
3. **Constraint encoding**: Explicit geometric constraints should be embedded in the generation pipeline to preserve design intent and enable parametric editing of critical dimensions such as chamber volume, orifice diameter, and wall thickness (dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 2-3, seff2021vitruvionagenerative pages 1-3).
4. **Feasibility checking**: A post-generation validation step—either solver-based or diffusion-guided self-repair—is essential to ensure watertight, manufacturable B-reps suitable for 3D printing (tsuji2025gencadselfrepairingfeasibilityenhancement pages 1-2).
5. **Open-source toolchain**: The GAD architecture (Python/Streamlit + OpenSCAD + GPT API) provides a practical starting template, while Seek-CAD demonstrates that locally deployed open-source reasoning models can achieve competitive performance without API costs (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 2-4, li2505seekcadaselfrefined pages 1-2).
6. **Training data**: The DeepCAD corpus (178k models) and Text-to-CadQuery annotations (170k pairs) provide the most immediately usable training resources, though domain-specific fine-tuning on mechanical/dosing-device geometries would likely be necessary (wu2021deepcadadeep pages 1-2, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 3-4).
answer.raw_answer:
## Literature Review: Generative CAD, AI-Assisted CAD, and LLM-Based Code Generation for Engineering Design (2021–2025)

### 1. Introduction

The rapid convergence of large language models (LLMs), deep generative architectures, and parametric computer-aided design (CAD) has created an emerging field of generative CAD that promises to transform engineering design workflows. This review surveys 15 key publications from 2021–2025 covering foundational datasets/benchmarks, text-to-CAD generation, constraint-aware sketch synthesis, agentic design loops with visual feedback, and the principal limitations of current approaches. Each entry is assessed for its relevance to building a Python/code-based generative CAD pipeline for an open-source powder-dosing device.

The following table provides a structured overview of all 15 references, organized by category, with summaries and relevance assessments.

| Reference # | Citation | Category | Summary (3-5 sentences) | Relevance to Python/Code-Based Generative CAD Pipeline for Powder-Dosing Device |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Wu et al. (2021) (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024) | Landmark Dataset / Foundational Model | Introduces the first deep generative network that explicitly outputs CAD construction sequences rather than discrete shape representations. Uses a Transformer-based autoencoder to encode and decode parameterized commands. Released a massive dataset of 178,238 CAD models paired with command sequences. | Provides the foundational sequence-based representation and dataset scale needed to train neural CAD engines. |
| 2 | Willis et al. (2021) (pqac-00000011) | Landmark Dataset / Benchmark | Introduces a large dataset of 8,625 human-designed CAD programs encoded as sketch-and-extrude sequences. Provides the Fusion 360 Gym execution environment that frames sequential construction as a Markov decision process. Enables benchmarking CAD reconstruction from target geometry using neurally-guided search. | Useful for understanding human design patterns and structuring reinforcement learning environments for code generation. |
| 3 | Seff et al. (2021) (pqac-00000028) | Constraint-Aware Sketch Generation / Dataset | Presents an autoregressive generative model for parametric CAD sketches that outputs both geometric primitives and explicit constraints. Trained on the SketchGraphs dataset comprising millions of real-world sketches. Uses self-attention to propagate state and outputs graphs compatible with standard constraint solvers. | Crucial for ensuring that generated 2D profiles for device components maintain valid geometric constraints before being extruded into 3D. |
| 4 | Para et al. (2021) (pqac-00000027, pqac-00000029) | Constraint-Aware Generation | A generative framework that outputs constrained sketches by decomposing the generation into primitives and their respective constraints. Uses a transformer architecture capturing long-range dependencies over a sequential token representation. Employs continuous parameter quantization followed by traditional constraint solvers to refine outputs. | Directly applicable for robustly generating and constraining 2D base profiles for mechanical parts. |
| 5 | Xie & Ju (2025) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) | Text-to-CAD Code Generation | Proposes generating executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language prompts using LLMs. Releases a massive dataset of 170k text-to-CadQuery pairs and fine-tunes models up to 7B parameters. Achieves a 69.3% exact match rate, demonstrating that scaling pretrained LLMs yields highly executable code. | Highly relevant as it directly outputs pure Python (CadQuery) scripts, perfectly aligning with a programmatic open-source CAD pipeline. |
| 6 | Wang et al. (2025) (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000032) | Text-to-CAD / Visual Feedback | An LLM-based text-to-CAD framework that integrates visual feedback to guide generation. Alternates between sequential learning on ground-truth parametric sequences and visual feedback stages using preference optimization (DPO). Rewards generated sequences that render into visually preferred models, overcoming the many-to-one rendering ambiguity. | Highlights how visual validation loops can improve the physical accuracy and visual fidelity of generated device components. |
| 7 | Govindarajan et al. (2025) (pqac-00000036) | LLM Fine-tuning / Evaluation | Reformulates CAD generation as a pure text-to-text task by fine-tuning code-LLMs (like Qwen2.5-Coder) on 170k models annotated with GPT-4.1. Uses a minimal JSON-based format to represent sequential CAD designs. Introduces advanced geometric and topological metrics (sphericity, mean curvature, Euler characteristic) to evaluate generated structures. | Demonstrates effective fine-tuning strategies for code-LLMs and provides rigorous metrics to validate generated mechanical parts. |
| 8 | Badagabettu et al. (2024) (pqac-00000038) | Agentic Design Loops | A zero-shot LLM framework that generates Python macros for FreeCAD based on natural language queries. Integrates a self-refinement loop that uses BLIP2 visual question-answering feedback to iterate on designs. Achieves a 53.6% success rate on the first attempt, improving by 23.1% after refinement iterations. | Directly matches the goal of creating a Python-based open-source CAD generator, providing a blueprint for agentic refinement loops. |
| 9 | Alam & Ahmed (2024) (pqac-00000035) | Image-to-CAD / B-rep Generation | An image-conditioned generative model that outputs parametric CAD command sequences for editable 3D shapes. Combines autoregressive transformers, a contrastive learning framework, and latent diffusion priors. Outperforms existing methods on unconditional and conditional CAD model generation tasks. | Demonstrates how multimodal inputs (like sketches or images of a powder device) can be translated into parametric code sequences. |
| 10 | Fan et al. (2025) (pqac-00000014, pqac-00000020) | Agentic CAD Design | An LLM-powered agent for conceptual CAD design that processes multimodal inputs and engages in interactive dialogue. Uses an Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) to decouple complex operations and stores cases in a knowledge base for improved generation. Integrates iterative visual feedback to refine the fidelity of the generated models. | Provides an architecture for an interactive, agentic design loop that can iteratively refine mechanical component code. |
| 11 | Li et al. (2025) (pqac-00000034) | Training-Free / Agentic Design | A training-free generative CAD approach using the locally deployed DeepSeek-R1 open-source model. Leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a CAD code corpus and a step-wise visual/Chain-of-Thought feedback loop to self-refine outputs. Introduces a new dataset organized around a Sketch-SketchFeature-Refinement (SSR) paradigm. | Highly relevant for building cost-effective, locally-hosted generative pipelines using open-source reasoning models. |
| 12 | Daareyni et al. (2025) (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000033) | Open-Source Pipeline / DfAM | Presents an open-source Python/Streamlit application linking GPT-4 with OpenSCAD for text/sketch-to-CAD workflows. Implements a three-layer closed-loop feedback mechanism involving syntax checks, LLM self-evaluation, and user feedback. Evaluates LLM-assisted CAD modeling for direct design-to-manufacturing applications. | Provides a practical, open-source architectural blueprint for translating AI outputs into manufacturable scripts. |
| 13 | Tsuji et al. (2025) (pqac-00000018) | Feasibility / B-rep Generation | Addresses the limitation that generative CAD models often produce infeasible boundary representations (~10% failure rate). Proposes a framework utilizing guided diffusion denoising and regression-based self-repair to correct faulty command sequences. Converts two-thirds of previously infeasible designs into viable, geometrically accurate models. | Critical for ensuring that AI-generated scripts for physical devices compile into manufacturable and watertight solids. |
| 14 | Heidari & Iosifidis (2025) (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000037) | Survey / Benchmark Overview | A comprehensive review of geometric deep learning applied to CAD. Details major benchmark datasets such as the ABC dataset (1M+ B-rep models), SketchGraphs (15M constraint graphs), and Fusion 360 Gallery. Highlights the shift from manual heuristics to automated feature representation and constraint synthesis. | Acts as a central reference point for understanding the landscape of datasets and benchmarks available for training CAD generators. |
| 15 | Dong et al. (2025) (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000031) | Dataset / Constraint-Aware Evaluation | Introduces a massive dataset of 170,236 executable constraint-aware parametric CAD sequences to bridge the gap between pure geometry and editability. Flattens sketch geometry into explicit geometric and dimensional constraint graphs. Presents a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark to evaluate how well models preserve design intent during edits. | Essential for ensuring that generated programmatic code includes valid constraints, keeping the open-source device editable and robust. |


*Table: A structured summary of key academic papers from 2021-2025 focusing on generative CAD, constraint-aware sketching, and LLM-driven code generation, mapped to their relevance for programmatic hardware design.*

### 2. Foundational Datasets and Benchmarks

**DeepCAD** (Wu et al., ICCV 2021) established the first large-scale dataset of 178,238 CAD models represented as parameterized command sequences (sketch + extrude operations). The Transformer-based autoencoder treats CAD operations analogously to natural language tokens, with continuous parameters quantized into 256 levels to convert regression into classification (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024). Evaluation metrics introduced include Chamfer Distance (CD), Command Accuracy (~99.3%), Parameter Accuracy (~97.3%), and Invalid Ratio (2.7–3.5%) (pqac-00000023). DeepCAD remains the most widely used training corpus for generative CAD research.

**Fusion 360 Gallery** (Willis et al., ACM TOG 2021) provides 8,625 human-designed CAD programs in a compact sketch-and-extrude language, alongside the Fusion 360 Gym interactive environment that exposes construction as a Markov decision process (pqac-00000011). The dataset also includes an assembly subset with 8,251 assemblies and 154,468 parts with joint annotations (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000012). It remains the primary benchmark for CAD reconstruction from target geometry.

**SketchGraphs** consists of 15 million real-world parametric 2D sketches from Onshape, each represented as a geometric constraint graph with nodes (primitives) and edges (constraints such as coincidence, tangency, and parallelism) (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000030). An open-source Python processing pipeline is provided. The **ABC Dataset** contributes over 1 million high-quality B-rep CAD models with precisely parameterized curves and surfaces, though it lacks construction histories (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009, pqac-00000010).

### 3. Text-to-CAD and LLM-Based Code Generation

The most directly relevant paradigm for a Python-based pipeline is **Text-to-CadQuery** (Xie & Ju, 2025), which generates executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language descriptions. The authors augmented the DeepCAD/Text2CAD data with 170,000 CadQuery annotations and fine-tuned six LLMs (124M–7B parameters), achieving a top-1 exact match of 69.3% and a 48.6% reduction in Chamfer Distance (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006). A fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini achieved a 0% invalid rate, demonstrating that larger models can produce fully executable code (pqac-00000005). This approach leverages the fact that foundation LLMs already excel at Python generation and spatial reasoning (pqac-00000006).

**CADmium** (Govindarajan et al., 2025) reformulates CAD generation as a text-to-text task by fine-tuning code-LLMs such as Qwen2.5-Coder using LoRA on 170k+ models annotated with GPT-4.1-generated descriptions. It uses a JSON-based format for CAD sequences and introduces geometric and topological evaluation metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic (pqac-00000036).

**Query2CAD** (Badagabettu et al., 2024) demonstrates a zero-shot approach where GPT-4 Turbo generates Python macros for FreeCAD without supervised training. It implements self-refinement loops using BLIP2 visual question-answering feedback, achieving 53.6% success on the first attempt and improving by 23.1% through iterative refinement (pqac-00000038).

**Daareyni et al. (2025)** present GAD, an open-source Python/Streamlit application integrating GPT-4 with OpenSCAD for multimodal text/sketch/voice-to-CAD workflows. The system implements a three-layer closed-loop feedback mechanism (syntax checking, LLM self-evaluation, and user feedback) and supports direct export to STL and G-code for manufacturing (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000033).

### 4. Visual Feedback and Agentic Design Loops

**CADFusion** (Wang et al., 2025) uses LLMs as the backbone and alternates between sequential learning on ground-truth parametric sequences and a visual feedback stage that rewards sequences rendering into visually preferred objects using DPO (direct preference optimization). This addresses the many-to-one rendering ambiguity inherent in CAD, where multiple parametric sequences can produce the same visual object (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000032).

**CADDesigner** (Fan et al., 2025) is an LLM-powered agent for conceptual CAD that accepts multimodal input (text + sketches), engages in interactive dialogue for requirement analysis, and supports a broad range of operations including extrusion, revolution, fillet/chamfer, sweeping, and lofting. It uses an Explicit Context Imperative Paradigm (ECIP) to decouple CAD operations and stores cases in a knowledge base for improved code generation (pqac-00000020, pqac-00000014).

**Seek-CAD** (Li et al., 2025) is a training-free approach using the locally deployed open-source DeepSeek-R1 model with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over a CAD code corpus. It employs step-wise visual feedback combined with Chain-of-Thought reasoning from a Vision-Language Model to iteratively self-refine generated parametric CAD code (pqac-00000034).

### 5. Constraint-Aware Sketch and Shape Generation

**SketchGen** (Para et al., 2021) addresses constrained sketch generation by decomposing the task into primitive generation and conditional constraint generation, p(S) = p(C|P)p(P), using two autoregressive Transformer networks. Continuous parameters are aggressively quantized during generation, then refined by a traditional constraint solver to correct discretization errors (pqac-00000027, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000029).

**Vitruvion** (Seff et al., 2021) similarly autoregressively generates both primitives and explicit constraints trained on SketchGraphs, but additionally supports conditioning on hand-drawn sketches for reverse engineering from noisy inputs. Its outputs are constraint-graph representations compatible with standard CAD constraint solvers, enabling import, solving, and subsequent editable modifications (pqac-00000028).

**HistCAD** (Dong et al., 2025) introduces 170,236 executable constraint-aware parametric CAD sequences in a CAD-backend-independent intermediate language. It explicitly encodes 19 constraint types and defines a Constraint-Aware Editability Benchmark with metrics for Edit Reachability (ER), Conditional Preserved Constraint Satisfaction Rate (cPCSR), and Overall Editable Success (OES), demonstrating that explicit constraints are essential for preserving design intent after parameter edits (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000031).

### 6. Image-to-CAD and B-rep Generation

**GenCAD** (Alam & Ahmed, 2024/2025) combines autoregressive transformers, contrastive learning, and latent diffusion priors to generate parametric CAD command sequences conditioned on images. It significantly outperforms prior methods on both unconditional and conditional generation tasks while also enabling image-based retrieval from large CAD databases (pqac-00000035).

**GenCAD-Self-Repairing** (Tsuji et al., 2025) addresses the critical limitation that approximately 10% of generated B-rep designs are infeasible. It proposes a guided diffusion denoising process in latent space combined with regression-based correction to repair infeasible command sequences, converting two-thirds of infeasible designs to feasible ones while maintaining geometric accuracy (pqac-00000018).

### 7. Survey Coverage

**Heidari & Iosifidis (2025)** provide a comprehensive survey of geometric deep learning for CAD, covering similarity analysis, retrieval, 2D/3D model synthesis, and generation from point clouds and images. The survey catalogs all major benchmark datasets—ABC (1M+ models), SketchGraphs (15M sketches), Fusion 360 Gallery—and notes that public CAD databases are predominantly unlabeled, posing obstacles for supervised learning (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000012, pqac-00000037).

### 8. Key Capabilities

Current systems demonstrate several capabilities relevant to building a generative CAD pipeline:

- **Text-to-CAD code generation**: Direct generation of executable Python (CadQuery, OpenSCAD, FreeCAD macros) from natural language, with best models achieving 69.3% exact match and 0% invalid rate at scale (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006).
- **Constraint-aware generation**: Explicit constraint graphs for sketch primitives enabling valid geometric relationships (parallelism, tangency, concentricity) that are essential for manufacturable parts (pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028, pqac-00000026).
- **Agentic design loops**: Self-refinement via visual feedback (VLM rendering + VQA scoring), Chain-of-Thought reasoning, and human-in-the-loop mechanisms that iteratively improve generated designs (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000034, pqac-00000038).
- **Design-to-manufacturing pipelines**: Open-source frameworks supporting direct export to STL, G-code, and connections to 3D printers (pqac-00000033).

### 9. Key Limitations

Several persistent limitations must be addressed for practical deployment:

- **Geometric hallucination**: LLMs may incorrectly generate circular profiles for hexagonal nuts, miss flange boreholes, or produce incorrect spring helices due to limited geometric reasoning (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000002).
- **Manufacturability gaps**: Generated models often lack validation for wall thickness, overhang angles, support structures, and other additive manufacturing constraints. Virtually approved designs can fail physically (pqac-00000015, pqac-00000014).
- **Parametric editability**: Training solely on parametric sequences may bias models toward training-set patterns, overlooking valid alternative constructions. Without explicit constraints, edits cause geometric distortion and loss of perpendicularity or tangency (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000026).
- **B-rep feasibility**: Approximately 10% of generated B-reps are infeasible, and the likelihood of invalid models increases with longer, more complex command sequences (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000013).
- **Evaluation challenges**: Standard metrics (Chamfer Distance, IoU) often fail to capture structural or topological quality. Newer metrics based on sphericity, mean curvature, and Euler characteristic are being introduced (pqac-00000036, pqac-00000023).
- **Dataset limitations**: Existing datasets are biased toward simple shapes, exclude certain CAD features (fillets, chamfers), and lack natural language annotations or manufacturing metadata (pqac-00000002, pqac-00000001).

### 10. Implications for a Python/Code-Based Generative CAD Pipeline for an Open-Source Powder-Dosing Device

For building a generative CAD pipeline targeting a powder-dosing device, several architectural recommendations emerge from this literature:

1. **Output representation**: CadQuery Python code is the most promising target, as it is a pure-Python, open-source API that does not require proprietary CAD software, and pretrained LLMs already demonstrate strong Python generation capabilities (pqac-00000006).
2. **Self-refinement**: Implementing an agentic loop with visual feedback (rendering the generated model and using a VLM to verify geometric fidelity) is critical, as first-attempt success rates remain modest (53–69%) but improve substantially with iteration (pqac-00000038, pqac-00000013).
3. **Constraint encoding**: Explicit geometric constraints should be embedded in the generation pipeline to preserve design intent and enable parametric editing of critical dimensions such as chamber volume, orifice diameter, and wall thickness (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000028).
4. **Feasibility checking**: A post-generation validation step—either solver-based or diffusion-guided self-repair—is essential to ensure watertight, manufacturable B-reps suitable for 3D printing (pqac-00000018).
5. **Open-source toolchain**: The GAD architecture (Python/Streamlit + OpenSCAD + GPT API) provides a practical starting template, while Seek-CAD demonstrates that locally deployed open-source reasoning models can achieve competitive performance without API costs (pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034).
6. **Training data**: The DeepCAD corpus (178k models) and Text-to-CadQuery annotations (170k pairs) provide the most immediately usable training resources, though domain-specific fine-tuning on mechanical/dosing-device geometries would likely be necessary (pqac-00000021, pqac-00000000).

Table comparisons show BrepGen (CVPR’24) outperforming DeepCAD (ICCV’21) on several unconditional-generation metrics (COV 71.26 vs 65.46, MMD 1.04 vs 1.29, JSD 0.09 vs 1.67, Novel% 99.8 vs 87.4, Unique% 99.7 vs 89.3, Valid% 62.9 vs 46.1). The paper defines evaluation measures used for generative CAD (Coverage, MMD, JSD, Novel, Unique, Valid) and for sequential CAD operation generation (ACCcmd, ACCparam, CD, Invalid Ratio). snippets: Wu et al. [10] proposed the first method to model 3D shape generation as a sequence of CAD operations.
The generative model uses chamfer distance (CD) as one of the evaluation metrics

Coverage (COV): The proportion of generated data points that have at least one reference data point that matches after assigning each generated data point to its nearest reference data point (based on chamfer distance).
Minimum Matching Distance (MMD): Measures the average distance between each data point in the reference dataset and its closest neighbor in the generated data.
Jensen–Shannon Divergence (JSD): Calculates the distribution difference between the generated data and the reference data after converting the point cloud data into discrete voxels.
Novel: The percentage of generated data that did not appear in the training set.
Unique: The percentage of data that appeared once when generated.
Valid: The percentage of generated B-rep data that is a sealed solid.
Chamfer Distance (CD): Calculates the distance metric between two sets of points.
Command Accuracy (ACCcmd): Measures the proportion of each operation in the generated CAD operation sequence that is correctly executed.
Parameter Accuracy (ACCparam): A measure of the closeness between the generated CAD operation parameters (such as size, position, etc.) and the real values.
Invalid Ratio: The proportion of the generated CAD model that cannot be successfully converted into a valid point cloud.

contexts.context: The excerpt compares several generative CAD and text-to-CAD approaches and highlights key methods, capabilities, evaluation setup, and limitations. Methods: Text2CAD is an end-to-end transformer-based auto-regressive model for parametric CAD from text; cadrille uses a two-stage pipeline (supervised fine-tuning on procedurally generated data + RL fine-tuning with online feedback); CADCodeVerify employs an agent using validation questions and visual feedback. Capabilities: CADDesigner generates structurally accurate models that better match image inputs by using expressive, high-level CAD operations (e.g., revolve, arrays/patterns) versus low-level extrusion-only operations. Evaluation: experiments used 1k samples from a filtered test set with abstract text prompts and image-based comparisons; metrics cited include SUC score and prompt-result alignment (CADDesigner outperforms others). Limitations: current systems struggle with precise global geometric constraints, spatial/topological reasoning, and complex mathematical geometry (e.g., involute gears); LLM/VLM and code-generation models have limited ability to produce correct spatial calculations and globally consistent designs. The excerpt does not provide details about DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, the ABC dataset, or a full Text2CAD benchmark description.

snippets: Text2CAD proposes an end-to-end transformer-based auto-regressive network to generate parametric CAD models from input texts.

cadrille proposes a two-stage CAD reconstruction pipeline: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on large-scale procedurally generated data, followed by reinforcement learning fine-tuning using online feedback.
CADCodeVerify proposes an agent that utilizes vali- dation questions and visual feedback for design verifi- cation.
For evaluation, we use 1k samples from the filtered test set described in Section 5.2. For each instance, we used the abstract text description as input prompts.
Our method demonstrates the best prompt-result alignment, followed by CADCodeVerify and Text2CAD.

While cadrille achieves a high SUC score, indicating it can produce syntacti- cally valid CAD models in most cases, the visualized results reveal these outputs are typically meaningless with respect to the input instructions

CADDesigner (blue) generates structurally ac- curate CAD models that closely match the input images by supporting expressive operations such as revolve and high-level patterns, whereas cadrille (green), relying on low-level extrusion, often produces syntactically valid but geometrically less faithful results.
expressive and semantically aligned CAD operations is crucial for faithful image-based CAD model generation.
Although our method demonstrates considerable flexibil- ity, it still faces challenges in generating models that require precise geometric constraints.
current LLMs and VLMs exhibit limited understanding and reasoning abilities of spatial geometric relationships and topological dependencies.
Due to limitations in the current code-generation models, the approach underperforms in generating CAD models involving complex mathematical computations, such as involute gears.

contexts.context: The excerpt frames text-to-CAD as generating CAD models from natural-language descriptions and critiques the dominant procedural paradigm (e.g., DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery) which records long operation sequences from CAD logs. Procedural methods are brittle: they are strictly order-dependent, prone to cumulative errors and cascading dependencies, and often include complex shapes that are hard to describe, reducing interpretability and evaluability. Procedural datasets also encode many low-level geometric constraints (parallelism, concentricity, symmetry) that are useful in interactive tools but largely redundant for code generation; instead, high-level semantic constraints (e.g., “two holes must be concentric”) better capture design logic. The paper proposes OBJ2CAD and a curated 1,000-example dataset emphasizing object-oriented, hierarchical representations that de-emphasize low-level constraints and retain semantic relations. Methodologically, it uses an LLM-driven pipeline combining top-down planning (task decomposition) with bottom-up generation (object instantiation), adds geometric assembly reasoning by casting assembly as explicit geometric-mathematical constraints, and supports an interactive iterative mechanism for user feedback and object-graph expansion. It also notes that LLMs exhibit object-centric reasoning, aligning with the proposed paradigm.

snippets: Text-to-CAD aims to generate CAD models directly from natural language descriptions.
Existing methods predominantly follow a procedural paradigm that represents modeling as long sequences of operations.
Most existing work relies on datasets such as DeepCAD (Wu et  al., 2021) and Fusion 360 Gallery (Willis et al., 2021), which are derived from logged modeling processes of CAD software
Such a paradigm is brittle: (1) procedural code strictly depends on the order of operations, making generation prone to cumulative errors and cascading dependencies

(3) procedural datasets often contain numerous complex shapes that are difficult to describe clearly in natural language (Wu et al., 2021; Willis et al., 2021), which reduces the interpretability and evaluability of text-to-CAD tasks;

they encode many low-level geometric constraints (e.g., parallelism, concentricity, symmetry), which are useful in interactive software for maintaining geometric relations but largely redundant in code-based generation
What is truly valuable are high-level semantic constraints, such as “two holes must be concentric” or “gear teeth counts must match”, which capture the logical dependencies between objects.
To support this shift, we curate a new dataset of 1,000 examples, including both industrial parts and carefully selected shapes that are linguistically describable.
Each example is converted into an object-oriented representation that emphasizes hierarchical structure and semantic constraints while de-emphasizing redundant low-level constraints.
We introduce OBJ2CAD, the first framework to shift text-to-CAD from a procedural to an object-oriented paradigm.
an LLM-driven framework that combines top-down planning with bottom-up generation
geometric assembly reasoning to formulate assembly explicitly as geometric-mathematical problems
an interactive iterative mechanism that incorporates user feedback to refine objects and expand the object graph
This hierarchical and modular representation more closely mirrors human design intuition
This shift is also consistent with recent studies that LLMs increasingly exhibit object-centric reasoning in complex tasks

contexts.context: The excerpt describes methods, datasets, and limitations relevant to generative CAD and text-to-CAD. DeepCAD uses a Transformer to generate CAD operation sequences, decomposing modeling into sketches and extrusion steps but producing disconnected sequences and supporting only basic operations. Text2CAD generates CAD sequences from text and visual features. Dataset-wise, ModelNet provides large 3D mesh models but lacks process data; the Fusion 360 Gallery adds modeling history and assembly data; the Mechanical Components Benchmark supplies complete modeling histories for mechanical parts but lacks natural language descriptions. The authors propose a CAutoD framework using LLMs and VLLMs to produce parameters and appearance descriptions, a Transformer-based CAD generator (TCADGen) with dual-channel feature aggregation, and CADLLM to refine sequences using TCADGen confidence scores. Key limitations noted: LLM-synthesized design data often lacks sufficient accuracy, LLMs are computationally expensive and inefficient, and it is difficult to guide LLMs with simple language or visuals to produce precise, editable CAD models. The excerpt does not provide details on SketchGraphs, ABC dataset specifics, the Text2CAD benchmark design, or concrete evaluation metrics.

contexts.context: The excerpt contrasts prior procedural text-to-CAD approaches with a proposed object-oriented alternative. Prior systems commonly use datasets derived from logged modeling sessions (cited examples: DeepCAD and Fusion 360 Gallery) and represent models as long ordered operation sequences. This procedural paradigm is described as brittle: strictly order-dependent, prone to cumulative errors and cascading dependencies, cluttered with redundant low-level geometric constraints (e.g., parallelism, concentricity, symmetry), and containing many shapes that are hard to describe in natural language. The authors argue that high-level semantic constraints (e.g., “two holes must be concentric”) are more valuable than low-level constraints for text-driven generation. To address these limitations, they introduce OBJ2CAD, including a curated object-oriented dataset of 1,000 examples, an LLM-driven framework combining top-down planning with bottom-up generation, explicit geometric assembly reasoning (formulating assembly as geometric-mathematical constraints), and an interactive iterative mechanism for user feedback and object-graph expansion. contexts.context: The excerpt describes several capabilities, methods, datasets, and limitations relevant to generative CAD. Generative AI in CAD is expanding beyond classic text/image/video/code categories to include 3D image generation and text-to-CAD modeling. One concrete method is generating CAD as a sequence of operations: DeepCAD uses a Transformer to predict construction sequences (2D sketches then 3D operations) and was trained on DeepCAD’s dataset of over 170,000 CAD models with their construction sequences. Outputs are parametric scripts yielding fully parametric models with clean, valid CAD features (e.g., flat planar faces, cylindrical holes). Commercial vendors are integrating AI (AutoConstraint, Project Bernini, PTC Creo Generative Design, SOLIDWORKS Design Assistant), but today most tools focus on optimizing existing workflows rather than fully autonomous model generation; Project Bernini is an example of a more generative effort not yet broadly available. A noted limitation is the gap between virtual validation and real-world performance: virtual experiments are cheap and popular, but virtually approved grasps have sometimes failed physically, underscoring the need for physical validation of generated designs.

snippets: Generative AI has been frequently divided into 5 categories.

emerging technologies are expanding these categories, introducing new categories and subcategories such as 3D image generation [34], text-to-CAD modeling [35, 4, 36], and synthetic data generation [37].
DeepCAD is the first generative model specifically targeting shapes as sequences of CAD operations.

The key idea behind DeepCAD is to autonomously generate commands describing how a CAD model is built, typically starting with a series of two-dimensional commands such as lines and circles, followed by three-dimensional operations such as extrusions.

The model was trained on DeepCAD’s own dataset comprising over 170,000 CAD models with their construction sequences, providing the model with examples of how designers typically build geometric structures.
the model’s output is a parametric script that represents a CAD model.
By generating CAD construction sequences, the approach produces fully parametric models.
The resulting geometries are clean, precise and have valid CAD features.
planar faces are flat, and holes are cylindrical.
Autodesk’s Project Bernini represents significant effort in generative AI for the design and make industries.
these industry leaders primarily leverage AI to optimize and streamline tasks within traditional modeling processes rather than generating entirely new models autonomously.
Virtual experiments have gained popularity in research due to their simplicity, speed and low cost.
grasp quality metrics and virtually approved grasps have failed in physical experiments.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes generative CAD methods that either output sequences of CAD operations (operation-sequence / procedural generators) or directly generate parametric/B-rep representations. Early work frames shapes as CAD operation sequences (Wu et al.), with architectures to improve hierarchy and structure (HNC) and end-to-end systems that infer sketches plus extrusion parameters (SecAD-Net). Other approaches assemble parametric geometry from surfaces (CAPRI-Net) or generate CSG programs from voxels (CSGNet). BrepGen is a diffusion-based model that directly generates B-reps, which are noted to be more challenging due to varied parameterized surfaces, curve/surface parameterization, and strict topological closure requirements. Key capabilities include controllable, hierarchical generation and direct conversion to meshes/point clouds; limitations include B-rep generation complexity, maintaining topological consistency, limited generalization and interpretability, and autoregressive sketch models’ error accumulation. Proposed remedies and future directions include integrating structural priors, multimodal fusion, design-rule constraints, layered/symbol semantics, and user-interaction mechanisms to improve modeling capability and engineering applicability.

snippets: Wu et al. [10] proposed the first 3D generative model that describes shapes as a series of computer-aided design (CAD) operations.
The deep generative model of CAD proposed by the authors is a series of operations used in CAD tools to construct 3D shapes.
Xu et al. [81] introduced a hierarchical neural encoder (HNC) architecture to achieve more refined and structured CAD model sequence generation.

Li et al. [26] designed an end-to-end method (SecAD-Net) that can automatically infer 2D sketches and their corresponding 3D extrusion parameters from the input original geometry, thereby realizing the reconstruction of CAD models.
Yu et al. [82] proposed CAPRI-Net, which adaptively learns and assembles parametric geometries from 3D surface data to generate CAD models.
CSGNet, which takes voxelized shapes as input and learns to generate a sequence of Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) operations.
Xu et al. [84] proposed BrepGen, a diffusion-based generative framework [85] that directly generates CAD models in the form of boundary representations (B-reps).
B-reps are more challenging to generate [86] than CSG or sketch representations
However, there are still deficiencies in generalization and interpretability [82,87].
However, the SketchGen model has the defect of autoregressive characteristics that prevents error correction in the early part of the sequence.

contexts.context: This excerpt describes Text-to-CadQuery, a paradigm that generates executable CadQuery code directly from natural language. The authors release a large dataset of 170,000 text–CadQuery pairs and show that using CadQuery as the output simplifies the pipeline and enables fine-tuning pretrained language models rather than training task-specific models. They evaluate models (ranging from ~124M up to 7B and including a fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini) using traditional core metrics plus two supplementary geometric metrics: a point-based F1 (10,000 sampled surface points, threshold τ=0.02) and volumetric IoU (voxelized in unit cube at resolution 0.02). Findings include strong scaling from small to 3B models (Qwen2.5-3B attains top F1/IoU medians), but the 7B Mistral underperforms in accuracy—likely due to insufficient data—while achieving the lowest Invalid Rate (1.32). A fine-tuned GPT-4.1 mini produced 0% Invalid Rate, showing larger models can produce fully executable code but still need adequate task data for precise 3D generation. Limitations noted: data scale/annotation effort, underfitting for larger models, and the need to test other input modalities (images, sketches, point clouds) with CadQuery outputs.

This model achieved a 0% Invalid Rate—producing fully executable code—but its accuracy matched that of the 7B model, confirming that advanced models are more reliable in execution but still require sufficient data to learn precise 3D generation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes the Text2CAD dataset and pipeline. The authors create a paired dataset of technical drawings and textual descriptions for 3D CAD models, using FreeCAD to render isometric and orthographic views (front, top, side) from imported STEP files. Text descriptions are generated with GPT-4 to avoid costly manual labeling and to capture detailed CAD semantics. The Text2CAD pipeline (Figure 2) first generates isometric images from text, then derives orthographic technical drawings from those isometrics, and finally reconstructs CAD models from the generated orthographic drawings. The work emphasizes the importance of technical-drawing standards and precise documentation for manufacturing. Overall, the excerpt documents dataset creation choices (STEP format, FreeCAD rendering, GPT-4 for descriptions) and a multi-stage generation/reconstruction workflow, but it does not provide details on other datasets (DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, ABC), evaluation metrics, model architectures beyond the pipeline steps, or quantitative limitations.

contexts.context: The excerpt proposes a Text-to-CadQuery paradigm: generate executable CadQuery Python code directly from natural language rather than intermediate, task-specific command sequences. CadQuery is advantageous because it is a high-level, pure-Python CAD API executable without external software, making outputs both runnable and interpretable. The authors augment the DeepCAD/Text2CAD data by annotating samples with CadQuery, producing ~170,000 text–CadQuery pairs to fine-tune pretrained LLMs. They fine-tune six models (124M–7B parameters) and observe a clear scaling trend: larger models yield better, executable CAD code. Reported improvements include a top-1 exact match increase (best model 69.3% vs 58.8%) and a 48.6% reduction in Chamfer Distance. The paper contrasts this code-first approach with prior methods that output command sequences or B-rep representations, which require task-specific decoders and added pipeline complexity. It also highlights that foundation models’ strengths in Python generation and spatial reasoning make this direct-code approach effective. The excerpt notes limitations are discussed later but does not provide details here. The excerpt does not include specific information about Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, ABC dataset, or a comprehensive list of evaluation metrics beyond exact match and Chamfer Distance.

The rise of foundation models, including large language and multimodal models, offers a promising path toward lowering this barrier by enabling more intuitive interaction with CAD systems through natural language, images, sketches, and point clouds [31, 9, 34, 1].

contexts.context: The excerpt describes sequence-based CAD generation (DeepCAD) and Transformer/LLM-driven approaches. DeepCAD’s dataset contains over 170,000 CAD models with construction sequences, enabling fully parametric, editable models with valid CAD features; however, it excludes some features (e.g., fillets) and cannot guarantee topological validity, especially for long/complex sequences. Text2CAD extends DeepCAD by annotating models with multiple natural-language prompts (via an LLM+VLM pipeline) and trains a Transformer to map text to construction sequences; its performance is limited by dataset biases toward simple shapes and missing feature types. LLM-based code generation writes scripts for systems like OpenSCAD and CadQuery; models (e.g., GPT-4) show surprising zero-shot competence for simple objects but struggle with complex geometry (examples: hex nut -> circular profile, missing flange boreholes, incorrect spring helix) due to lack of geometric reasoning. Improvement strategies include visual-feedback/refinement loops such as CADCodeVerify (VLM renders model, iteratively questions and refines code) and hybrid workflows where GPT-4 produces design docs, structured JSON, and selects scripting functions, with final rendering in OpenSCAD.

Many modern CAD systems, such as OpenSCAD [38] and CadQuery [39], support creating models through code or a scripting language.

Initial results from Li et al. [40] suggest that LLMs have surprising competence, even in zero-shot settings, producing syntatically correct code and generating geometrically reasonable models for simple objects.
For instance, when prompted to create a hexagonal nut, an LLM may incorrectly generate a circular profile instead.
Without specific training or geometric reasoning, LLMs lack an understanding of spatial relationships and other 3D constraints, they just predict statistically likely sequences of text
CADCodeVerify by K. Alrashedy et al. [43]
CADCodeverify functions as a code refinement mechanism that enables a VLM to visually inspect the generated model and provide corrective feedback.
This is achieved through an iterative question generation and answering process
One key challenge is in the natural flexibility of language.
The LLM (GPT-4) is tasked with three key subtasks: parametric computation, instruction sequence construction, and coding model scripting.
Once the script is completed, it is rendered using OpenSCAD to produce the final 3D model.

contexts.context: The paper surveys generative CAD approaches, available datasets, and how large pretrained models enable text-to-CAD generation. Early CAD datasets were largely static B-rep models without modeling steps; more recent datasets include command sequences and paired natural-language annotations. Representative datasets and sizes include ABC (B-rep, 1,000,000+), SketchGraphs (command sequences, 15,000,000+), DeepCAD (command sequences, ~178k models), Fusion360 Gallery (command sequences, 8,625) and Text2CAD (extends DeepCAD with natural-language; resulting corpus ~170k models and >600k prompts). Methods differ by input modality (text, images, sketches, point clouds) and output representation (command sequences, B-rep, or script-based targets like CadQuery). Prior symbolic-code efforts include Query2CAD (57 samples, FreeCAD macros) and LLM4CAD (~5,000 CadQuery samples but limited to five part types). The authors emphasize using text-only inputs and leveraging foundation LLMs (GPT-4, Gemini) for spatial reasoning and code synthesis, generating accurate and executable CadQuery code. Evaluation focuses on executability and scaling behavior across model sizes; limitations include small or narrow datasets and early-stage, not-yet-specialized models, motivating larger, more diverse annotations and systematic evaluation.
contexts.question: What are the key capabilities, methods, datasets, and limitations of generative CAD, text-to-CAD, and LLM-based CAD code generation systems? Include details about DeepCAD dataset, Fusion 360 Gallery, SketchGraphs, Text2CAD benchmark, ABC dataset, and evaluation metrics.

contexts.context: From the excerpt: ABC (2019) is a very large CAD benchmark ("more than 1 million individual and high-quality CAD models") with parametric descriptions of surfaces and curves that "can generate data in different formats and resolutions so that various geometric learning algorithms can be compared fairly" (download: https://deep-geometry.github.io/abc-dataset). DeepCAD (2021) contains 178,238 items and is annotated with "sequence annotation" for procedural/operation-sequence tasks (download: https://github.com/ChrisWu1997/DeepCAD). The Fusion 360 Gallery set is provided in two related releases: a Reconstruction split (2021) with 8,625 items and "Build history" annotations, and an Assembly split (2022) listing "8251 Assemblies /154,468 Parts" with "Assembly information"; both are available at the Autodesk AILab GitHub (https://github.com/AutodeskAILab/Fusion360GalleryDataset). SketchGraphs (2020) supplies sketches and "diagram annotation" and is indicated as having sequence/sketch annotations (download: https://github.com/PrincetonLIPS/SketchGraphs). The excerpt presents these datasets as sources for CAD-focused learning tasks and benchmarks—ABC explicitly for fair comparisons across geometric learning methods, Fusion360 for reconstruction/assembly and build-history tasks, DeepCAD for sequence-based annotations, and SketchGraphs for sketch/diagram-based learning.

Sequence representation records these geometric operations in the order of execution to form a set of operation instructions.

Sequence representation is a way to express the “construction history” of CAD models, which facilitates neural network learning and modeling, and is suitable for model generation and reconstruction tasks.
A two-dimensional CAD sketch is represented as a graph structure where nodes represent geometric elements and edges represent the relationships between geometric elements.

In the SketchGraphs dataset [113], each CAD sketch can be represented as a graph structure, where nodes represent geometric elements (such as lines, circles, etc.) and edges represent constraints or relationships between these elements.
This section systematically reviews the current mainstream open-source datasets in the CAD field.

contexts.context: From the excerpt: DeepCAD is a large CAD command-sequence dataset (178,238 CAD models) where each model is represented as a CAD command sequence; it was created to train an autoencoder and its scale and command-sequence representation make it suitable as a benchmark for learning generative or reconstruction models that require operation histories. The Fusion 360 Gallery reconstruction dataset is an artificial-design dataset (8625 design sequences/CAD programs) using B-Rep and recording clear construction history (sketch+extrude workflows), intended for ML on parametric CAD reconstruction. The Fusion 360 Gallery assembly dataset provides assembly-level data (8251 assemblies, 154,468 parts) plus joint data (32,148 joints between 23,029 parts) following the Fusion 360 API; its joint/constraint annotations make it a benchmark for assembly and connection-understanding tasks. SketchGraphs contains 15 million parametric CAD sketches extracted from Onshape, each with a geometric constraint graph; it is aimed at sketch-level, parametric tasks (e.g., patch segmentation, normal estimation). By contrast, the ABC dataset provides B‑Rep geometry but lacks operation/command sequences, limiting its use for benchmarks that require construction histories.
contexts.question: What are the details of DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery dataset, SketchGraphs dataset, and ABC dataset for CAD machine learning? What are their sizes, representations, and how are they used as benchmarks?

snippets: Wu et al. [10] proposed a new CAD command sequence dataset to train their proposed autoencoder.
This dataset contains 178,238 CAD models and their CAD construction se- quences.
Each model in the dataset is represented as a CAD command sequence.
Willis et al. [9] proposed the Fusion 360 Gallery reconstruction dataset, which is the first artificial 3D CAD geometry dataset designed for machine learning.
The dataset contains 8625 artificial design sequences (i.e., CAD programs), which are simple to operate and only involve sketching and extrusion modeling.

The Fusion 360 Gallery reconstruction dataset uses the B-Rep format, which not only facilitates fine control of 3D shapes but also records a clear design construction history, providing valuable information for analysis and modeling.

Willis et al. [120] proposed the Fusion 360 Gallery assembly dataset, which contains a large number of CAD assemblies and provides detailed information about joints, contacts, and underlying assembly structures.

The dataset is based on designs in Autodesk Fusion 360 and is divided into two parts: the assembly data, which includes 8251 assemblies and 154,468 parts, and the joint data, which contains 32,148 joints between 23,029 parts.
Seff et al. [113] proposed a dataset of 15 million sketches extracted from real-world CAD models: the SketchGraphs dataset.
Each sketch in the dataset is accompanied by a real geometric constraint graph representing its configuration.
The models in the SketchGraphs dataset are parametric CAD models that can support tasks such as patch segmentation and normal estimation.
Compared with the ABC dataset, the CADs in the ABC dataset are provided in B-rep format, and there is not enough information to recover how the CAD operations built the design.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes DeepCAD, a Transformer-based generative autoencoder that represents 3D shapes as sequences of CAD operations (e.g., curve sketch, extrusion, fillet, boolean, chamfer) where each operation is controlled by discrete and continuous parameters. DeepCAD both encodes CAD command sequences into a latent space and decodes latent vectors back into CAD operation sequences, enabling tasks such as shape autoencoding and random shape generation. To train the model the authors created and publicly released a new dataset of CAD construction sequences: "a new CAD dataset consisting of 178,238 models and their CAD construction sequences." They emphasize that this dataset is orders of magnitude larger than previous CAD-command-sequence datasets. Outputs from DeepCAD are CAD-command sequences that can be imported into standard CAD tools (e.g., SolidWorks, AutoCAD) or converted into polygon meshes and point clouds, making the generated designs editable and practical for downstream use. The paper positions this work as the first generative model that directly produces CAD designs rather than discretized 3D representations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes three major CAD datasets used in geometric deep learning. ABC: a large-scale, real-world B-Rep-style dataset with over 1 million high-quality 3D CAD models. Models are represented as precisely parameterized curves and surfaces (suitable for sharp feature representation, patch segmentation, analytic differential measurements, and shape reconstruction). ABC models were collected via an openly accessible Onshape interface and include a geometry-processing pipeline for ML use, but construction history is only accessible via Onshape’s proprietary API (making reconstruction tasks harder). SketchGraphs: a collection of 15 million human-designed 2D sketches from Onshape, where each sketch is represented as a geometric constraint graph (nodes = 2D primitives, edges = constraints like coincidence/tangency). It is targeted for tasks such as sketch auto-completion, autoconstraint prediction, CAD inference from images, and learning semantic sketch representations; an open-source Python data-processing pipeline is provided. Fusion 360 Gallery: introduced by Autodesk as the first human-designed 3D CAD dataset that includes 3D operation sequences for programmatic CAD construction; it ships with an open-source Python environment (Fusion360 Gym) for processing CAD operation sequences. The excerpt does not mention DeepCAD. Note: many large-scale CAD databases (e.g., ABC) are predominantly unlabeled, which limits some supervised benchmark uses.
contexts.question: What are the details of DeepCAD, Fusion 360 Gallery dataset, SketchGraphs dataset, and ABC dataset for CAD machine learning? What are their sizes, representations, and how are they used as benchmarks?

snippets: ABC [77] is the first large-scale, real-world, and hand designed dataset with over 1 million high-quality 3D CAD models covering a wide range of object types.

Each CAD model consists of a set of precisely parameterized curves and surfaces, offering accurate reference points for sharp geometric feature representation, patch segmentation, analytic differential measurements, and the process of shape reconstruction.

CAD models in ABC dataset are compiled and collected via an openly accessible interface hosted by Onshape [40], and an open-source geometry processing pipeline has been developed to process and prepare the CAD models in ABC dataset to be used by deep learning methods.

The construction history information in ABC dataset can only be retrieved by querying the Onshape API in a proprietary format with limited documentation which makes it challenging to develop CAD (re-)construction methods on that dataset.
SketchGraphs dataset has been produced to fill this gap by providing a collection of 15 million human-designed 2D sketches of real-world CAD models from Onshape API.

2D sketches representing geometric primitives (like lines and arcs) and constraints between them (like coincidence and tangency) can be seen as the basis of 3D shape parametric construction, and each 2D sketch is presented as a geometric constraint graph where nodes denote 2D primitives and edges are geometric constraints between nodes (3).

The SketchGraphs dataset can be used for various applications in automating design process such as auto-completing sketch drawing by predicting sequences of sketch construction operations or interactively suggesting next steps to designer, and autoconstraint application where the method is predicting a set of constraints between geometric primitives of sketch.
An open-source Python pipeline for data processing and preparation for deep learning frameworks also comes along the dataset.
Fusion 360 Gallery [30] has been introduced recently by Autodesk as the first human designed 3D CAD dataset and environment with 3D operation sequences for programmatic CAD construction.
The fusion 360 Gallery dataset comes along an open source Python environment named as Fusion360 Gym for processing and preparing the CAD operations for machine learning
the majority of large-scale public databases, such as ABC, remain predominantly unlabeled.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes current AI capabilities and limits for CAD generation relevant to LLM-based approaches. Major vendors are embedding AI to automate tasks (e.g., AutoConstraint) and research projects (Autodesk’s Project Bernini) can generate 3D shapes from minimal inputs (images, text, voxels, point clouds). Research directions include LLMs and diffusion-based B-rep generative methods. A sequence-of-operations approach (DeepCAD) trains Transformers on construction sequences (trained on over 170,000 CAD models) and outputs parametric scripts that yield clean, precise geometries with valid CAD features; such outputs can be opened and modified in CAD software, supporting parametric editability. However, industry adoption today focuses more on augmenting traditional workflows than fully autonomous modeling. Accuracy and manufacturability concerns are highlighted by the mismatch between virtual validation and real-world performance—virtually approved grasps have failed physically, implying risks of hallucinated or non-manufacturable features and the need for physical validation. Overall, the text indicates promising parametric generative capabilities but also accuracy, validation, and practical deployment limitations.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and limitations of LLM-based CAD generation including hallucination of features, manufacturability concerns, parametric editability, accuracy issues, and agentic design loops with visual feedback?

snippets: AutoConstraint, which analyzes CAD sketches to automatically identify and apply geometric relationships, ensuring that designs remain fully constrained.

Project Bernini represents significant effort in generative AI for the design and make industries. The initial model can swiftly generate functional 3D shapes from various inputs, including 2D images, text descriptions, voxels and point clouds.

Autodesk is pursuing more AI research, focusing on generative models for CAD applications. This includes LLMs [30] and diffusion based generative methods for boundary representation (B-rep) CAD modeling [31].

major companies in the CAD modeling industry are actively integrating AI into their software to improve existing design workflows. Currently these industry leaders primarily leverage AI to optimize and streamline tasks within traditional modeling processes rather than generating entirely new models autonomously.
DeepCAD is the first generative model specifically targeting shapes as sequences of CAD operations.
The model’s output is a parametric script that represents a CAD model.

The model was trained on DeepCAD’s own dataset comprising over 170,000 CAD models with their construction sequences, providing the model with examples of how designers typically build geometric structures.
The resulting geometries are clean, precise and have valid CAD features.
allows users to open the generated models in CAD software and modify
grasp quality metrics and virtually approved grasps have failed in physical experiments. Therefore physical experiments are essential to ensure the practicability of the designed fingers.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes capabilities and limits of generative CAD approaches (GenCAD and the proposed GenCAD-Self-Repairing). Generative models can produce CAD programs/command sequences and be enhanced by techniques like guided diffusion denoising in latent space plus regression-based self-repair to improve feasibility. A reported baseline failure mode is that roughly 10% of generated B-reps are infeasible; the self-repair pipeline converted two-thirds of those infeasible designs to feasible ones while "maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models." Key limitations stem from the nature of CAD: B-reps encode parametric surfaces, edges, and vertices, requiring editable and manufacturable representations and precise topological/parametric relationships, which are "challenging to process directly with standard neural network architectures." The domain also suffers from limited large-scale, structured datasets (though ABC contains ~1M B-reps), and the integration of generative AI with CAD modeling remains limited. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss hallucinated features or agentic visual-feedback design loops.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and limitations of LLM-based CAD generation including hallucination of features, manufacturability concerns, parametric editability, accuracy issues, and agentic design loops with visual feedback?

snippets: a major limitation of GenCAD is its inability to consistently produce feasible boundary representations (B-reps), with approximately 10% of generated designs being infeasible.
CAD models require an editable and manufacturable representation
The industry-standard boundary representation (B-rep) [11] encodes parametric surfaces, edges, and vertices, making CAD models more structured but also more complex than other 3D formats.

This hierarchical nature, combined with the need for precise topological relationships and parametric geometry, makes B-rep data challenging to process directly with standard neural network architectures.
the integration of generative AI with CAD modeling remains limited.
To address this limitation, we propose GenCAD-Self-Repairing, a framework combining a guided diffusion denoising process in the latent space and a self-repair pipeline for infeasible CAD sequences.
Our approach successfully converted two-thirds of infeasible designs in the baseline method into feasible ones
maintaining a reasonable level of geometric accuracy between the point clouds of ground truth models and generated models.
One major bottleneck in this field is the lack of large-scale, structured datasets for training AI models capable of generating realistic CAD designs.
ABC dataset is the largest, containing approximately one million CAD models

contexts.context: The paper describes a framework that integrates large language models (notably GPT-4/GPT-4v/GPT-4o) with CAD tools to accept multimodal inputs (text, blueprints, images, voice) and generate CAD code and models. It emphasizes lowering barriers to 3D modeling and uses a closed feedback loop for iterative refinement of generated models. The authors cite prior work (LLM4CAD) that evaluates outputs with parsing rate (syntax/parse success) and intersection-over-union (geometric overlap) to quantify accuracy and geometry fidelity. Approaches to improve parametric editability and robustness include AIDL, a solver-aided domain-specific language that offloads precise geometric computation to a constraint solver and supports implicit dependencies, explicit constraints, and hierarchical structure, and CAD-Llama, which uses structured code formats and hierarchical annotation to adapt LLMs for parametric CAD. The excerpt highlights LLM strengths (code generation, debugging, multimodal understanding) and shows how metrics and solver-integration address accuracy and editability. The excerpt does not explicitly discuss hallucination of features or detailed manufacturability validation, though it notes that a "significant degree of experience and proficiency remains essential" for viable CAD design.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and limitations of LLM-based CAD generation including hallucination of features, manufacturability concerns, parametric editability, accuracy issues, and agentic design loops with visual feedback?
